Set up online/app banking; link PayPay/Rakuten Pay to the account.
Buy a commuter pass (teiki-ken) on your Suica once your regular route is set — ~30% cheaper than per-ride.
Book Ghibli Museum August tickets on Lawson at 10:00 JST on July 10 — set the alarm now. You're now operational: foundation done before the 14-day deadline.

top10.tip: Treat the first 14 days as a single legal sprint: address registration → pension (+ exemption) → NHI → My Number → bank, in that dependency order
top10.reason: Address registration is the master key that unlocks everything else, and the 14-day window is a hard deadline with a fine up to ¥50,000. Doing pension/NHI in the same ward visit saves trips and backdating headaches.

top10.tip: Enroll in National Pension AND immediately file the exemption (免除) in the same motion
top10.reason: As a 20-59 resident it's legally mandatory regardless of visa, but with zero prior-year Japan income you almost certainly qualify for full/partial exemption — pausing the ~¥17k/month bill while staying compliant. Don't rely on the Canada-Japan treaty; it only covers employer-detached workers.

top10.tip: Skip the tax-free myth — use point systems instead for real savings
top10.reason: WHV holders are NOT eligible for tax-free shopping (it needs temporary-visitor status), so you pay the 10% consumption tax like a resident. The real savings levers are point ecosystems (PayPay/Rakuten/dPoint), used-gear shops, and furusato-nozei once you're earning.

top10.tip: Set up the money stack in order: Chase Sapphire (no-FX Visa) in Apple Pay → passport-only SIM (Japanese number) → PayPay → one points ecosystem (Rakuten)
top10.reason: A Japanese phone number gates every money app, and your Chase Sapphire (no-FX) in Apple Pay covers tap-pay + most purchases. Foreign-card Mobile Suica top-up can be finicky — test it day one; Wise (optional) or a Japanese card is the reliable Suica fallback. Concentrating spend in one ecosystem (Rakuten for gear) beats spreading thin.

top10.tip: Skip normal apartments; use a no-key-money share house (Oakhouse/Sakura House/Borderless House) as your first base
top10.reason: Normal Tokyo apartments stack key money + deposit + agent fee + a guarantor you can't provide — easily ¥400-600k upfront. Share houses skip all four, you can apply before you fly, and move in within your first week for ~¥120-180k all-in.

top10.tip: Run your year on the festival/seasonal calendar — book the lottery/timed-entry items the day their window opens
top10.reason: The highest-value experiences (Ghibli monthly drops, Disney Fantasy Springs/Vacation Packages, sumo, hanami timed entry, Comiket/TGS) sell out in minutes or have fixed monthly release dates. Calendar-driving beats reacting.

top10.tip: Anchor convention season Jul-Sep: Comiket C108, Wonder Festival, Maker Faire, Tokyo Game Show 30th
top10.reason: This four-event cluster hits every interest (anime, figures, DIY/electronics, games) and all fall in your first three months while you still have novelty energy. Buy advance tickets to skip brutal same-day lines and summer heat.

top10.tip: Plan a quiet-weekday Tokyo Disney run in early July (your arrival weeks are genuinely low-crowd), then again Sep-Oct
top10.reason: DisneySea's 25th 'Sparkling Jubilee' runs Apr 2026-Mar 2027 — the headline event of your stay. Tue/Wed/Thu outside school breaks plus rope-drop + free Standby Pass + targeted DPA gets you the Fantasy Springs headliners without paying for everything.

top10.tip: Budget for the year-2 tax tail: ¥0 residence tax in 2026, but a first bill ~June 2027 on your 2026 Japan earnings
top10.reason: Residence tax is assessed on the prior year and billed from June, so if you depart mid-2027 around billing, settle it or appoint a tax representative so it doesn't chase you. Same logic for claiming the pension Lump-Sum Withdrawal (within 2 years, appoint a rep to reclaim the 20.42% withholding).

top10.tip: Lock a coworking/work-café routine and a commuter pass early to make remote work sustainable
top10.reason: As a builder on a 12-hour-offset timezone, a stable async work rhythm and a teiki-ken commuter pass (~30% cheaper than per-ride between two stations) are what turn 'visiting Tokyo' into 'living and working in Tokyo' for a full year.

findings.why: Processing is fast (7–15 business days) but document prep is the real bottleneck.
findings.how: Passport, application form + photo, CV, written activity plan, proof of funds bank statement, flight itinerary.

findings.tip: Japan Post Bank opens for new arrivals
findings.why: Most banks demand 6 months residency; Japan Post does not.
findings.how: Bring residence card + registered address (住民票). Visit a post office branch.

findings.tip: Japanese credit card is effectively impossible on a WHV
findings.why: No local credit history; banks decline.
findings.how: Use your Chase Sapphire Preferred (no foreign-transaction fee) for purchases + a no-FX debit card for ATM cash as the bridge. Rakuten is the eventual long shot.

findings.tip: Share houses skip key money + guarantor
findings.why: A normal lease wants 3–5 months rent upfront plus a guarantor.
findings.how: Oakhouse (largest), Sakura House (newcomer-focused), Borderless House (intl mix), Social Apartments (nicer). Many let you book from abroad.

findings.tip: Start around the Chuo line west or Setagaya
findings.why: Central access, lower cost, social, instant address.
findings.how: Nakano / Koenji / Higashi-Nakano, or Sangenjaya / Shimokitazawa. Kichijoji if you'll pay a bit more for livability.

findings.tip: Data-only eSIM + authenticator app beats a virtual number
findings.why: Most travel eSIMs have no phone number; SMS 2FA is the only thing that breaks.
findings.how: Buy an eSIM (Ubigi/Airalo) ~5 days before flying; move 2FA to an authenticator app or keep CA SMS roaming as backup.
findings.impact: ~$10–20 vs paying for an unused virtual number

findings.tip: Skip tourist IC cards — you're a resident
findings.why: Welcome Suica etc. are for tourists.
findings.how: Add Suica to Apple Wallet or get a personalized commuter pass (定期券) once you know your route.
findings.impact: Commuter pass can save 30%+ on a fixed route

findings.tip: Install Japan's disaster apps + learn 119 / 110 in week one
findings.why: Tokyo gets frequent quakes; you arrive mid-tsuyu into typhoon season. Knowing the system before you need it matters.
findings.how: Install JNTO 'Safety Tips' (multilingual quake/tsunami/weather push alerts) and NHK World. Emergency numbers: 119 fire/ambulance, 110 police. Keep a small go-bag (water, light, power bank, copies of passport + residence card).
findings.impact: You react correctly in the first 60 seconds of a quake or evacuation order.

findings.tip: Learn your ward's garbage sorting + collection days immediately
findings.why: Sorting is strict and per-ward; wrong bags get left (with a sticker) and can sour share-house relations.
findings.how: Get your ward's English garbage guide (burnable, non-burnable, recyclable PET/cans/bottles, oversized = yuryo sticker). Buy the designated bags at any conbini/supermarket. Note collection mornings — put it out by ~8:00, not the night before.
findings.impact: No rejected trash, no friction with housemates/neighbours.

findings.tip: Register any bike you buy/receive (防犯登録) — it's mandatory
findings.why: Police do spot-checks; an unregistered bike (or one still in the seller's name) can be treated as stolen.
findings.how: Anti-theft registration (bouhan toroku) is ~¥600 at the bike shop on purchase. If given a used bike, transfer registration with the previous owner's details. Carry ID when riding.
findings.impact: Avoids an awkward (or worse) police stop.

findings.tip: Know how to actually see a doctor on NHI before you're sick
findings.why: There's no GP-gatekeeper system; you pick a clinic directly, but English-friendly ones are limited.
findings.how: With NHI you pay 30% at any clinic/hospital. Use the Tokyo 'Himawari' medical info service (multilingual) or JNTO's clinic finder to find English-speaking clinics. Pharmacies (薬局) fill prescriptions; bring your insurance card every visit.
findings.impact: You get care fast without overpaying or language panic.

items.task: Confirm WHV eligibility (age 18–30, Canadian citizen; visa already granted)

ADHD meds — carry your NCD (Narcotics Control Department) import permit + 3-month supply. Japan classes lisdexamfetamine (Vyvanse) as a 'Stimulants' Raw Material'; the NCD advance-import permit carried WITH your prescription is the legal route. Adult domestic refills are not available in Japan — plan resupply for months 4-12 (verify with your prescriber).
Gather visa documents: passport, form + photo, CV, written activity plan
items.note: Document prep takes 1–3 months — start early.

items.task: Show proof of funds in your account
items.note: ~CAD $3,500 with a return ticket, ~CAD $4,500 without. Your flight is one-way → budget higher or add a changeable return.

items.task: Book consulate appointment and submit application
items.note: Processing ~7–15 business days. Visa must be in your passport before you fly.

items.task: Check passport validity + blank pages

(Optional) Wise account — you're going Chase-Sapphire-only, so this is an upgrade, not required: Wise is the most reliable way to top up Mobile Suica and gives cheap mid-market JPY. Skip it to keep things simple.
items.note: Bridges banking until Japan Post is set up; cheap FX.

items.task: Move 2FA to an authenticator app (don't rely on SMS abroad)
Reserve a furnished share house (book from abroad if possible)
items.note: Gives you an instant registrable address on arrival.

items.task: Buy a data eSIM (~5 days before flying)
Fill out Visit Japan Web for fast NRT arrival

Before you fly: buy and install a landing-day eSIM (Ubigi or Sakura Mobile) on your iPhone/Android, but do NOT activate it until you land. This gives you instant data the moment you step off the plane at NRT, before you deal with any long-term SIM. Aim for a 10-15GB or unlimited 15-30 day plan to bridge your first weeks. Keep it un-activated in case your flight changes.
items.note: eSIM works without any Japanese card - you pay with your Canadian card at purchase. Ubigi is rated best overall for coverage incl. rural; Sakura has the best English support. Cost roughly CAD 15-35 for the bridge period. Activate only after landing. Confirm your phone is carrier-unlocked and eSIM-capable.

items.task: Book first-week accommodation BEFORE landing - do not arrive without a reservation. Best strategy for a 12-month WHV base-building in Tokyo: book 5-10 nights at a hotel or hostel near a hub (Shinjuku/Shibuya/Ueno) as a soft-landing buffer while you view share houses in person. Budget hotel ~6,000-12,000 yen/night; hostel dorm ~3,000-5,000/night. This avoids committing to a room sight-unseen.
items.note: Have a confirmed booking printout for landing (immigration occasionally asks where you're staying). A hotel/hostel buffer beats locking a 1-year lease blind. NRT to central Tokyo: Keisei Skyliner ~2,500 yen / ~45 min, or cheaper Access Express.

items.task: Line up a no-key-money share house (Oakhouse / Sakura House / Borderless House) as your real first base instead of a normal Japanese apartment. These skip the four classic rental gotchas: NO key money (reikin, normally 1-2 months non-refundable), NO guarantor (hoshonin - nearly impossible as a new arrival), NO agent fee, and little/no deposit. You can apply online before you fly and reserve a room before you land. Typical Tokyo all-in: ~50,000-90,000 yen/month rent + a one-time contract fee (~30,000-50,000 yen) + monthly utilities/maintenance fee, furnished with wifi included.
items.window: apply 2-4 weeks before landing; move in within first week
items.note: Oakhouse explicitly advertises no deposit, no key money, no agent fee, no guarantor, furnished + wifi. This is THE workaround for the guarantor/key-money trap that blocks most newly-arrived foreigners from normal leases. A regular apartment can demand 4-6 months' rent upfront plus a Japanese guarantor.

items.task: (Optional, for a Chase-Sapphire-only setup) Order a Wise card and verify the account while still in the US, before you fly. A Wise card issued in your home country (CA/US) keeps Apple Pay/Google Pay compatibility; a Wise card issued after you become a Japan resident does NOT work with Apple Pay/Google Pay, so you lose the ability to top up a mobile Suica with it. Load some JPY at the mid-market rate so you land with spendable funds.
items.note: Wise uses the mid-market rate, no monthly/minimum-balance fees; ~74% of transfers arrive in under 20 seconds. CRITICAL gotcha: order the card in Canada to preserve Apple Pay support. Card takes days to mail, so do this well before the 30th.

items.task: Decide your long-term plan BEFORE arrival: a WHV holder cannot get a Japanese credit card or major carrier (docomo/au/SoftBank/Rakuten) contract on day one because they reject foreign cards and want a residence card. Pre-order a foreigner-friendly SIM from Mobal (no residence card needed, real 070/080/090 number, free worldwide shipping, pays with your home (CA/US) card) so a physical SIM is waiting or shippable. Sakura Mobile and GTN are alternatives but lean toward needing a residence card / convenience-store payment.
items.note: A real Japanese phone number matters: you need one to open a bank account, sign an apartment lease, and register for services. Mobal ~JPY 2,200/mo, Sakura ~JPY 3,000/mo, GTN ~JPY 2,000/mo (voice). Mobal is the lowest-friction for a freshly-landed WHV with only a passport. You can later switch to a cheap MVNO (IIJmio/povo/LINEMO) or a big carrier once you have your residence card and a Japanese bank account/card.

items.task: Confirm the proof-of-funds figure per your Canadian WHV terms (already cleared at application). Canadian WHV: most recent bank statement showing roughly CAD 3,500 if you hold a return/onward ticket, OR ~CAD 4,500-5,000 if you do NOT. Print a fresh bank statement (dated within the last few days) and carry a paper copy. Note: this is checked at APPLICATION (already done if visa is in hand), but also keep it on you for landing.
items.note: The CAD 3,500 / 4,500 split and the return-ticket condition are stated by the Japanese consulates in Canada. Exact figure varies slightly by consulate (Vancouver vs Calgary vs Toronto) and can change - verify with the consulate that issued your visa. Bring a freshly-dated printed statement.

items.task: Decide the one-way vs return-flight question deliberately. Japanese immigration almost never asks WHV holders for an onward ticket at the border (the visa IS your permission to stay 12 months), BUT the AIRLINE can deny boarding at NRT departure check-in, and holding a return/onward ticket lets you use the lower CAD 3,500 funds tier. Cheapest compliant move: buy a flexible/refundable onward ticket or a cheap throwaway flight out of Japan, OR ensure your bank balance clears the higher (~CAD 4,500-5,000) no-ticket threshold.
items.note: WHV does not legally require onward proof at immigration, but airlines enforce onward-travel rules more strictly than immigration. Carrying a ticket also drops your required proof-of-funds. A fully refundable fare or a low-cost LCC ticket out (e.g., to Seoul) satisfies airline check-in.

items.task: Collect your Residence Card (zairyu card / 在留カード) at the Narita immigration desk on arrival. Show your passport with the WHV visa; the officer prints and hands you the card on the spot (issued at NRT, HND, Chubu, Kansai only). The back is blank until you register an address — keep it on you at all times, it is your legal ID in Japan.
items.note: Carrying the card is legally required 24/7. If for any reason it is not issued at the airport, it will be mailed after you register your address at the ward office.

items.task: Carry proof of funds + onward planning docs through immigration. Your Canadian WHV required showing sufficient funds (commonly ~CAD 2,500, or ~CAD 4,000 without a return ticket) at visa stage; keep a printed bank statement and your return/onward ticket evidence accessible at Narita in case the immigration officer asks.
items.note: Verify closer — exact proof-of-funds figures are set at the consulate/visa stage and rarely re-checked at the border, but having the statement and onward-ticket evidence on hand removes any risk of a secondary-inspection delay.

items.task: Set up Mobile Suica in Apple Wallet on your iPhone before/at arrival and top it up from your Chase Sapphire Preferred in Apple Pay (a no-FX Visa). Heads-up: foreign-card Mobile Suica top-up is sometimes rejected — test it on day one; if CSP won't top up, a Wise card or a Japanese card is the reliable fallback. This is your IC transit + tap-to-pay card from minute one — no deposit, works nationwide. Avoid the physical Welcome Suica (only 4 weeks) and physical Suica deposits.
items.window: before departure or day of landing
items.note: iPhone Mobile Suica is the simplest IC setup. Android phones bought outside Japan usually lack FeliCa and can't run Mobile Suica — if on Android, plan to buy a physical IC card instead. A View card (JR East) for auto-charge needs a Japanese credit card, which you won't have on arrival; revisit later if you stay long.

items.task: Withdraw your first JPY cash at a Seven Bank (7-Eleven) ATM in the Narita arrivals hall using a foreign card, NOT the airport currency-exchange counters. Seven Bank ATMs give near-mid-market rates and accept foreign cards 24/7; exchange counters have worse spreads. Japan is still heavily cash-friendly for small shops, so carry some yen.
items.note: Expect a small ATM fee (~110-220 yen depending on amount/card network). Pull enough for the first week (rent deposits, conbini, small restaurants). Tell your Canadian bank you're travelling so the card isn't blocked.

items.task: Maintain your proof-of-funds bank balance until your visa is granted and you've landed — do NOT spend it down for the application. The Canadian Working Holiday requirement is roughly CAD 3,500 in your account PLUS either a return ticket or an extra ~CAD 1,000 (so ~CAD 4,500 with no return ticket). Keep a bank statement showing this.
items.window: through application and entry (now to landing)
items.note: Threshold varies by which consulate you applied through (Vancouver/Toronto/Calgary differ slightly). Confirm the exact number on YOUR consulate's page. Border officers can ask to see funds on entry, so keep a recent statement accessible when you land at Narita.

items.task: At Narita on landing day: rely on your pre-installed eSIM first - it is free of queues. Only visit an airport SIM/wifi counter (Terminals 1 and 2 have SoftBank Global Rental / AnyFone, Ninja WiFi, Sakura) as a backup if your eSIM fails. Skip buying a pricey walk-up tourist SIM at the airport - those are short-term tourist data SIMs with no Japanese number and walk-up prices are higher than online.
items.note: Airport counters are convenient but the cheapest path is eSIM now + your pre-arranged long-term SIM. Pocket-wifi pickup at the airport is also possible if you skipped the eSIM. As a solo remote worker an eSIM/SIM beats pocket wifi - see the pocket-wifi item.

items.task: Get your Residence Card (zairyu card) ON ARRIVAL at Narita. As a WHV holder landing at NRT, immigration issues the residence card at the airport during landing permission. Confirm the card prints correctly and keep it on you at all times - it is the master key that unlocks address registration, banking, phone contracts, and work.
items.note: Residence card is issued at major airports (Narita, Haneda, Kansai, Chubu) on entry. By law you must carry it at all times. Address is blank on issue - you fill it in at the ward office (next task).

items.task: Break the SIM/bank/address chicken-and-egg with a passport-only SIM. Banks and most contract phone plans demand a Japanese phone number AND a registered address - but you can't get a contract SIM without an address. Solution: buy a Mobal or Sakura Mobile SIM/eSIM that needs ONLY your passport (no residence card, no address) - order before departure or pick up at Narita. This gives you the Japanese number you need to open the bank account and fill out forms from day one.
items.window: order before departure or buy at NRT on arrival
items.note: Mobal explicitly issues a real Japanese phone number with passport only, no residence card required - this is the standard hack to bootstrap the system. Once your residence card has an address, you can later switch to a cheaper long-term contract SIM (IIJmio, Rakuten, etc.).

items.task: Keep a printed funds-and-status packet on your person for landing day (not just in checked luggage): passport with WHV sticker, residence card (issued at counter), fresh bank statement clearing the proof-of-funds threshold, accommodation booking confirmation, and onward/return flight (if you chose to hold one). Immigration is usually quick for WHV but can spot-check funds and where you'll stay.
items.note: Verify closer - WHV holders are rarely deep-questioned, but having the packet ready avoids secondary inspection delays. Digital copies on your phone as backup, but officials prefer paper.

items.task: Budget the realistic move-in cash you need liquid in your first month. Share-house route (recommended): first month rent + one-time contract fee + maintenance/utility fee, roughly 120,000-180,000 yen all-in for a Tokyo Oakhouse-style room - far less than a normal apartment. AVOID normal apartments at the start: they stack key money (1-2 months, non-refundable) + deposit (1-2 months) + agent fee (1 month) + guarantor-company fee, easily 400,000-600,000 yen upfront and usually a Japanese guarantor you can't provide.
items.note: Carry/access enough for share-house move-in PLUS ~2-4 weeks living costs before your Japanese bank account and first paycheck land. International ATM withdrawal or a card that works at 7-Eleven/Japan Post ATMs covers the gap before your Yucho account opens. Yucho generally cannot send/receive INTERNATIONAL transfers in your first ~6 months — use Wise for inbound CAD→JPY early on.

items.task: File your moving-in notification (転入届 / 住民登録) at your Tokyo ward or city office once you have a fixed address. Bring passport + Residence Card; they print your address on the back of the card on the spot and create your resident record (juminhyo). This is the legal gateway that unlocks pension, health insurance, My Number, and banking — do it first.
items.window: within 14 days of settling into your address
items.note: Hard 14-day legal deadline under the Resident Registration Act; late filing can trigger penalties. The clock runs from when you move into your address, not from landing — but with a 6/30 arrival, plan for the 7/14 outer limit. Bring your lease/address details. Do pension + NHI + My-Number registration in the SAME visit.

items.task: Enroll in the National Pension (国民年金, Category-1 insured) at the pension/insurance counter during your ward-office visit. As a 20–59 WHV resident this is legally mandatory regardless of visa type. Do NOT assume the Canada–Japan totalization agreement exempts you: the certificate-of-coverage exemption only applies to workers DETACHED/posted by an employer (and who had 6+ months prior home coverage), which does not fit a typical WHV maker.
items.window: same visit as address registration (within 14 days)
items.note: 2026 monthly contribution is roughly ¥17,000+/month. Canada-Japan agreement (in force since 2008) helps you TOTALIZE years toward a Canadian/Japanese pension later, but its exemption route is for posted employees, not WHV. Confirm your exact situation at the counter.

items.task: Apply for a National Pension contribution exemption (免除 menjo) at the SAME counter where you enroll. As a newcomer with zero prior-year Japan income you are very likely to qualify for full or partial exemption — you can enroll AND apply for exemption in one motion. This legally pauses the ~¥17k/month bill while keeping you compliant.
items.note: Exempted months still count proportionally toward future totalization (a full-exempt month counts less than a paid month for benefit amount). Tell the clerk you just arrived with no Japan income. This is the single biggest money-saver for WHV makers — ~¥200k/yr avoided. Exemption is income-tested on prior-year income; raise it at the counter — outcome is likely but not guaranteed.

items.task: Enroll in National Health Insurance (国民健康保険 / NHI) at the ward office during the same registration visit — mandatory for any resident on a 3+ month visa. Bring Residence Card + passport. Coverage backdates to your move-in date, so enrolling promptly avoids a lump retroactive bill.
items.window: within 14 days of becoming eligible (same ward visit)
items.note: Premiums are income-based: a newcomer with no prior-year Japan income pays roughly ¥1,500–4,500/month the first year; covers 70% of medical costs. From Dec 2025 plastic insurance cards are no longer issued — your My Number Card doubles as the insurance card once registered (a paper certificate is issued in the interim).

items.task: Get a Japanese phone number (SIM/eSIM) immediately — it's the gating dependency for almost all money apps. PayPay, Rakuten Pay, bank apps, and point programs all require a Japanese number for SMS verification. Without it you cannot register the cashless payment ecosystem.
items.note: A Japanese number unblocks PayPay/Rakuten Pay/bank apps. Some setups also ask for a residence card or My Number. From June 2026 new/renewed residence cards integrate the My Number on the same card — verify what your card shows.

items.task: iPhone users: set up transit IC same day. Easiest path on a foreign iPhone is the Welcome Suica Mobile app (English, accepts foreign cards, free, no Japanese Apple ID, valid 180 days) OR add a Suica directly in Apple Wallet (+ button > Transit Card > Suica). Tap to ride trains, subway, buses, konbini immediately - no physical card or queue needed.
items.note: Critical gotcha: foreign-issued VISA cards are blocked by the Visa network for Apple Pay Suica top-ups. Use a Mastercard or Amex for in-Wallet recharge, or top up with cash at any konbini. Suica and PASMO are interchangeable across Tokyo. Welcome Suica Mobile is tourist-oriented (180-day) and easiest now; a regular Mobile Suica never expires and is better long-term once you have a Japanese card.

items.task: Android users: check FeliCa/Osaifu-Keitai support BEFORE relying on Mobile Suica. Most non-Japan Android phones (incl. global Pixels and pre-2027 Galaxy) cannot add Suica in Google Wallet - it simply won't appear. If yours is unsupported, carry a physical Suica/PASMO card instead and top it up with cash.
items.note: Mobile Suica on Android needs a Japan-market FeliCa phone. Good news for later: Samsung Galaxy adds out-of-the-box Suica support for all models from 2027 (first half). Until then, if you are on an imported Android, a physical IC card is the reliable choice. Verify your exact model's compatibility before assuming it works.

items.task: Register your address at the local ward/city office within 14 DAYS of landing - this is a hard legal deadline. Bring your residence card and passport, file the Moving-In Notification (tennyu todoke), and they will write your address on the back of your residence card. While there, also request a few copies of your Juminhyo (residence certificate ~300 yen each) - you'll need it for the bank. Missing the 14-day window risks a fine up to 50,000 yen.
items.note: This is the linchpin that breaks the address chicken-and-egg: share house move-in gives you an address, ward office stamps it onto your residence card + issues Juminhyo, and THEN banks/phone contracts become possible. Also enroll in National Health Insurance and National Pension here at the same visit.

items.task: Lock in long-term housing before the Sakura House (Makoto) stay ends on Jul 7 — view share-house rooms in person, then sign.

Open a Japanese bank account. Japan Post Bank (Yucho) accepts new arrivals without a 6-month-residency requirement and has the most ATMs nationwide — best first account. Bring your residence card (issued at Narita) and proof of a Tokyo address once you have one. Online banks Sony Bank and Rakuten Bank offer English apps and debit cards as good second accounts.
items.window: within 2-4 weeks of landing (after address registration)
items.note: GOTCHA: Japan Post Bank won't let you do overseas deposits/remittances if your stay is under 6 months — so don't rely on it for Canada<->Japan transfers early on; use Wise for that. Most banks want a Tokyo address (so register at your ward office first) and sometimes a Japanese phone number. A hanko/seal is often optional at Yucho.

items.task: Set up PayPay as your primary QR cashless wallet — it's the most widely accepted code-payment app in Japan and the lowest-friction to start (link a credit card or charge via bank). Register with your Japanese phone number; have your residence card ready for identity verification if prompted.
items.note: PayPay = broadest small-merchant acceptance and frequent cashback campaigns. You can fund it with a foreign credit card initially; link a Japanese bank account later for bank-charge and to receive PayPay-point promotions.

items.task: Pick ONE points ecosystem to concentrate spend and avoid spreading thin. For your profile (online shopping for gear, broad acceptance): Rakuten (Rakuten Pay + Rakuten Bank + Rakuten Ichiba for synths/electronics) earns Rakuten Points across the whole ecosystem. dPoint (docomo) is strong if you use docomo mobile. Keep PayPay for in-person QR. Rakuten Pay registration needs a residence card + Japanese phone number.
items.note: Concentrating spend in one ecosystem maximizes point yield. Rakuten is the most foreigner-practical all-in-one (bank + pay + shopping). dPoint pays off mainly with a docomo line. Exact 2026 point rates/campaigns change often — verify current earn rates closer before committing.

items.task: Open a bank account at Japan Post Bank (Yucho) once you have: residence card WITH printed address + Juminhyo + Japanese phone number. Japan Post Bank is the most new-arrival-friendly major bank - it does NOT strictly enforce the 6-month-residency rule that megabanks impose, so WHV holders can open early. Go to a post office branch with all three documents. This account is what employers pay wages into and what you use for rent/utilities.
items.note: Megabanks (MUFG, Mizuho, SMBC) often refuse foreigners with under 6 months' residence. Japan Post Bank is the standard go-to for WHV holders. Bring your Inkan/hanko stamp if you have one (some branches accept signature). Required: residence card, address-matching Juminhyo, JP phone number. Yucho generally cannot send/receive INTERNATIONAL transfers in your first ~6 months — use Wise for inbound CAD→JPY early on.

items.task: Primary card = Chase Sapphire Preferred (Visa, no foreign-transaction fee). Set a travel notice and add it to Apple/Google Pay. Note: CSP at an ATM is a cash-advance (fee + interest) — use it for purchases, not yen withdrawals.
Sort a cash/ATM card for Japan — Japan is cash-heavy and CSP charges a cash-advance fee. Bring a no-FX debit card (or the optional home-issued Wise card) for Seven-Bank ATM withdrawals.
Plan ADHD-med resupply for months 4-12 — no adult refills in Japan (mail-import with a fresh NCD permit, a trip home, or a prescriber arrangement).

Watch your mailbox for the My Number notification (個人番号 assigned automatically when you register your address) — a letter arrives at your registered address ~2-3 weeks after move-in. Your number is the key ID for health insurance, tax, and employment. Then optionally apply for the physical My Number Card (separate application, online/post/in-person; ready in ~2-4 weeks).
items.window: letter arrives ~2-3 weeks after address registration; card application anytime after
items.note: The number itself is auto-assigned — no action needed to receive it. The physical card is optional but increasingly useful: from Dec 2025 it serves as your health-insurance card, and from 14 June 2026 the new Tokutei Zairyu Card lets some residents update Residence Card + My Number Card in one procedure. Employers will ask for your My Number.

items.task: Skip the hanko/inkan for now — it is NOT required to open accounts at the foreigner-friendly banks (Japan Post, Sony Bank, SBI Shinsei, Rakuten, SMBC Prestia all accept a signature in 2026). Only legacy megabanks (MUFG, Mizuho) may still ask for a seal on some forms. Buy a cheap name-seal later only if an employer or landlord specifically demands one.
items.note: A custom katakana/romaji hanko runs ¥1,000-3,000 at Don Quijote/Hanko-ya if ever needed. Most WHV makers never register a formal jitsuin. Yucho generally cannot send/receive INTERNATIONAL transfers in your first ~6 months — use Wise for inbound CAD→JPY early on.

items.task: Open a Japanese bank account AFTER your Residence Card shows your registered address (you cannot open one before). For a WHV/short-residency holder the realistic options are Japan Post Bank (Yucho) — most branches, often accepts 3+ months' residency — and SBI Shinsei Bank, historically the most foreigner-friendly and openable soon after arrival. Sony Bank is good for FX/remote-work income but can be stricter on short visas.
items.window: after address is printed on Residence Card; within first 2-4 weeks
items.note: WHV-specific gotcha: the Foreign Exchange Control Law's 6-month rule means many banks refuse residents with under 6 months in Japan — Yucho and SBI Shinsei are the standard workarounds. Expect extra scrutiny for temporary visas. Bring Residence Card + passport + a Japanese phone number (get a SIM first). Yucho + Shinsei combo gives you cash-everywhere ATMs plus easy English online banking. Yucho generally cannot send/receive INTERNATIONAL transfers in your first ~6 months — use Wise for inbound CAD→JPY early on.

items.task: Budget the 10% consumption tax — there is no tax-free shopping for you on a WHV
items.note: Tax-free shopping in Japan requires 'temporary visitor' status; a Working Holiday visa is a residence status, so you are not eligible (even on day one). Ignore any '<6 months' or 'before Dec 2026' advice — it doesn't apply. Plan gear/electronics budgets with the 10% in.

items.task: Use Wise (not your Japanese bank, and not Japan Post Bank early on) for all Canada<->Japan transfers all year. Mid-market rate, fees shown upfront, cheaper the more you send (e.g., ~CAD 203 to move CAD 40,000). Set up the recipient details once so future top-ups of your JPY balance or sends back to Canada take seconds.
items.note: Wise holds a Japan Type-1 Funds Transfer license. Far cheaper than bank SWIFT wires (which add intermediary + receiving fees and poor FX). Watch CAD/JPY rate and move money when favorable rather than in many small chunks.

items.task: Plan furusato nozei (hometown tax) ONLY if you'll actually owe Japanese resident/income tax. You qualify regardless of nationality IF you're paying Japanese income/resident tax and are registered as a resident on Jan 1, 2027. Resident tax is assessed on Jan-1 residency, so donations made in calendar year 2026 offset 2027 resident tax. If you work little and owe little/no tax, there's nothing to offset — skip it.
items.note: You land June 30, so you'd be a resident on Jan 1 2027 — eligibility hinges on having taxable Japanese income. WHV part-time income may be low, so run a furusato-nozei calculator (donation cap scales with income) before donating. Use the One-Stop Exception (no tax return) only if you have <=5 donation municipalities and no other filing need. Verify your taxable-income estimate closer.

items.task: Pocket wifi - mostly skip it as a solo remote worker. It makes sense only if you want one device covering laptop+phone+gear without tethering, or as a stopgap before home internet is installed in your apartment. Ninja WiFi is ~JPY 440/day pre-booked (more walk-up); a monthly unlimited home/portable wifi can bridge you until fixed fibre is installed (installs often take 2-4 weeks).
items.window: evaluate within first 2 weeks if home internet is delayed
items.note: For one person, eSIM/SIM tethering usually beats carrying and charging a separate router. Keep pocket wifi in mind specifically for the apartment-internet gap, since Japanese fibre installation can lag weeks behind move-in. Verify current daily/monthly rates closer to arrival.

items.task: Get a physical Suica or PASMO card if (a) you are on an unsupported Android, or (b) you want a cash-toppable card independent of any credit card. Buy from ticket machines at JR (Suica) or Tokyo Metro/private rail (PASMO) stations. A physical card also lets you load a commuter pass even if your phone can't.
items.note: Physical cards matter when: your phone lacks FeliCa, you prefer cash top-ups, or a service/venue still expects a card. Both Suica and PASMO work network-wide across Tokyo trains, subways, buses, and konbini. Top up with cash at any station machine or konbini register - no credit card needed.

items.task: Practice IC top-up habits: keep a balance buffer (JPY 2,000-3,000) so you never get gate-stopped. Cash top-up works at any station fare-adjustment/charge machine and at konbini (7-Eleven, Lawson, FamilyMart) registers. In-app/Wallet top-up needs a Mastercard/Amex (not foreign Visa). Auto-charge exists but generally requires a Japanese-issued card.
items.note: Cheapest and most reliable as a new arrival: cash top-ups at konbini. Switch to card/auto-charge once you have a Japanese bank account and card. Foreign-Visa block applies to recharge via Apple Pay specifically.

items.task: Once your home base and any regular commute/coworking route is fixed, buy a commuter pass (teiki-ken) loaded onto your Suica/PASMO. It gives unlimited travel between two stations for 1, 3, or 6 months, and you ride free at every station in between. Buy at station machines/ticket offices or in the Mobile Suica/PASMO app; longer terms = bigger discount (often ~30% vs per-ride).
items.window: after you settle a routine (likely month 1-2)
items.note: Only worth it if you make the same trip regularly (e.g., to a coworking space, language school, or recurring meetup hub). As a remote worker with a variable schedule, do the math: if you don't commute a fixed route 15+ days/month, pay-per-ride may be cheaper. The pass also covers off-peak/weekend trips on that route at no extra cost.

items.task: Plan your SIM upgrade path for ongoing savings: after you have your residence card + a Japanese bank account/card (usually weeks 2-6), migrate from Mobal/Sakura to a cheaper MVNO (IIJmio, povo, LINEMO, mineo) to cut your monthly bill, keeping your number via MNP if you want to retain it.
items.window: weeks 4-8, after bank account + residence card
items.note: Foreigner-friendly SIMs cost more for the convenience of day-one access without a Japanese card. Once you can pass standard KYC (residence card) and pay with a Japanese card, MVNOs drop you to ~JPY 1,000-2,000/mo. Confirm MNP/number-portability rules with your provider before switching. Verify specific MVNO plans closer to the switch date.

See a big hanabi (fireworks) show
items.note: Sumida River & others run mid–late summer.

items.task: Eat kakigori (shaved ice) on a brutal humid day
Go to Tokyo DisneySea — ride Fantasy Springs

Try seasonal foods: sanma, chestnuts, new-crop rice
Catch a sumo basho if one's running
teamLab (Borderless or Planets)
Winter illuminations (start early Dec)
Tokyo Disneyland Christmas season
Hatsumode (first shrine visit) Jan 1
Eat osechi / New Year foods
Mid-year admin check: residence tax, pension on track
Optional: snow trip / onsen weekend
Hanami — cherry blossoms (~late Mar–early Apr)
items.note: Ueno, Meguro River, Shinjuku Gyoen, Chidorigafuchi.

items.task: Hanami picnic with conbini + depachika snacks
AnimeJapan (spring anime convention)
A spring road/rail trip while the weather's perfect
NEVER leave Japan without a re-entry permit (single-entry WHV)
items.note: Tick the special re-entry box at the airport — leaving without it voids your visa.

items.task: If you DO pay into the pension during your stay (6+ contributing months), file for the Lump-Sum Withdrawal Payment (脱退一時金) AFTER you permanently leave Japan and surrender your address. You must claim within 2 years of departure or the right expires. A flat 20.42% income tax is withheld; appoint a Japan tax representative before leaving to reclaim much of that withholding.
items.note: Only relevant if you actually paid (i.e. did NOT take full exemption). The 20.42% withholding is partly recoverable by filing a Japanese tax return via a designated tax agent. File the agent appointment (納税管理人) before you depart.

items.task: Plan for residence tax (住民税) — you owe ZERO in your first Japanese year. Residence tax is assessed on the PRIOR calendar year's income and billed from June. With no 2025 Japan income, you get no 2026 bill. Your first residence-tax bill (~June 2027) will be based on your 2026 Japan earnings, so budget for it — and if you depart Japan mid-2027 before/around billing, settle or appoint a tax representative so the bill doesn't chase you.
items.note: Verify closer — exact 2027 billing depends on your 2026 income and your January-1 2027 registered municipality (residence tax is owed to wherever you were registered on Jan 1). If leaving Japan before paying, you must appoint a 納税管理人 (tax representative) or prepay. Roughly ~10% of taxable income.

items.task: Track Japanese pension (and its refund) as part of your money plan. If you're enrolled in Japanese pension during work, you can claim a Lump-Sum Withdrawal Payment after you leave Japan — but a flat ~20.42% income tax is withheld unless you appoint a Japanese tax representative to reclaim it. Decide on a tax representative BEFORE you depart Japan to recover that withholding.
items.window: set up rep before leaving Japan (~mid-2027); claim refund after departure
items.note: Canada-WHV gotcha: the lump-sum pension refund is taxed ~20.42% at source; a tax representative files to reclaim it after you leave. The refund itself is capped (limited number of contribution months). Pension enrollment rules for WHV part-timers vary by employer/hours — confirm your enrollment status and the current refund mechanics closer to departure.

oneTime.label: Flight (YVR→NRT, one-way)
oneTime.note: Off-peak one-way economy runs ~¥80–110k; book early. Verify closer.

oneTime.label: WHV application
oneTime.note: Free for Canadians at the embassy/consulate.

oneTime.label: Move-in (deposit + first month, foreigner-friendly share house)
oneTime.note: Foreigner-friendly operators waive key money/guarantor; budget ~one month rent + a low contract/deposit fee.

oneTime.label: SIM / eSIM setup
oneTime.note: Prepaid data eSIM on arrival, then a longer plan once you have an address.

oneTime.label: Bedding / starter kit
oneTime.note: Futon/bedding, kitchen basics, hangers — most share houses are furnished but bedding is on you.

monthly.label: Rent (share house)
monthly.note: Foreigner-friendly share houses run ~¥45–95k/mo; private room in a Tokyo hub.

monthly.label: Utilities / internet
monthly.note: Often a flat ~¥10–22k/mo in share houses (electric/gas/water/wifi bundled).

monthly.label: Transit (Suica)
monthly.note: Daily commuting + getting around; a commuter pass can lower this.

monthly.label: Food & groceries
monthly.note: Mix of supermarket cooking and cheap eats (¥400–800 chain bowls). Eating out more pushes this up.

monthly.label: Phone plan
monthly.note: Budget MVNO data plan once you have a Japanese address/bank.

monthly.label: NHI + National Pension
monthly.note: National Health Insurance + National Pension (Category-1). Apply for the pension exemption (免除) to lower this; first-year NHI is income-assessed. Verify at the ward office.

monthly.label: Fun / travel
monthly.note: Shows, arcades, gear, day trips — the part of the year you actually came for.

packing.item: Passport (6+ months validity) + WHV visa page
packing.note: Immigration checks the visa sticker on arrival — keep the passport on you, not in checked luggage.

packing.item: Certificate of Eligibility / visa docs (printed)
packing.note: Bring a paper copy even if you have a digital one — kept with your immigration paperwork.

packing.item: Travel medical insurance proof (covers until NHI kicks in)
packing.note: National Health Insurance enrolment happens after you register your address; insure the gap.

packing.item: Passport-size photos (4+) for forms & residence card
Driver's licence + International Driving Permit (if you'll drive)
Copies/scans of all key docs (cloud + offline)
packing.note: Email yourself PDFs and keep an offline copy in case you lose the originals.

packing.item: 2+ cards (Visa/Mastercard) incl. a no-FX-fee card
packing.note: Carry two networks in case one is declined; a no-foreign-transaction-fee card saves real money.

packing.item: Starter ¥ cash (¥30,000–50,000) for day one
packing.note: Many small shops, the ward office and some transit top-ups are cash-only; get yen before you fly or at the airport.

packing.item: Tell your bank you're moving abroad (avoid fraud blocks)
Pocket wifi or eSIM for arrival connectivity
packing.note: Set up an eSIM before landing, or reserve pocket wifi for pickup at the airport — you'll need data day one.

packing.item: Power: Japan is Type A, 100V (North-American plugs fit; check voltage)
packing.note: North-American plugs physically fit, but Japan is 100V vs 120V — confirm dual-voltage on chargers/appliances.

packing.item: Power bank (carry-on only — never checked)
packing.note: Airlines require lithium power banks in carry-on; check the Wh limit.

packing.item: Unlocked phone + charger
Laptop + charger (for remote work / setup)
Prescription meds + Yakkan Shoumei if over the import limit
packing.note: Some common OTC meds (certain decongestants like pseudoephedrine, codeine) are restricted or banned — verify each before bringing.

packing.item: Doctor's letter / prescription copy for any medication
Small first-aid kit + any preferred OTC remedies
Deodorant & specific toiletries (harder to find / differ in Japan)
packing.note: Western-style deodorant and some skincare are limited; bring a starter supply.

packing.item: Spare glasses / contacts + prescription
Light, breathable layers for a humid late-June arrival
packing.note: Tokyo in late June is hot and humid heading into rainy season — pack for sweat, not the airport's AC.

packing.item: Compact umbrella / rain jacket (tsuyu rainy season)
Comfortable walking shoes (easy to slip off)
packing.note: You'll walk a lot and remove shoes indoors often — slip-on friendly helps.

packing.item: Extra shoes/clothes if you're a larger size (harder to find)
packing.note: Larger Western shoe and clothing sizes can be hard to buy locally.

packing.item: One smart/business-casual outfit (ward office, interviews, dining)
Carry-on with passport, cash, meds, charger, change of clothes
packing.note: Assume checked bags could be delayed — keep everything you need for 24h on you.

packing.item: First-night address + arrival card details written down
packing.note: You fill in a disembarkation/landing card; have your accommodation address handy.

packing.item: Plan for Suica/PASMO (IC transit card) on arrival
packing.note: Add a digital Suica to your phone wallet or buy a physical card at the airport for trains.

packing.item: Inkan / hanko personal seal (optional but handy)
packing.note: A name stamp can smooth bank/lease paperwork; not strictly required but sometimes requested.

packing.item: Foldable tote / reusable bag (shops charge for plastic)
Small hand towel (public restrooms often lack dryers/paper)
Small Canadian gifts (omiyage) for new contacts/hosts

numbers.label: Police
numbers.note: Crime, theft, accidents, a lost or stolen residence card. Free from any phone.

numbers.label: Fire / Ambulance
numbers.note: Say 火事 (kaji, fire) or 救急車 (kyūkyūsha, ambulance). Free; the operator may have limited English — keep your address handy.

numbers.label: Coast Guard
numbers.note: Sea and coastal emergencies, accidents on the water.

numbers.label: Japan Helpline (English, 24h)
numbers.note: Free English-language emergency support line. Not a substitute for 110/119 in a life-threatening emergency.

contacts.label: Embassy of Canada to Japan (Tokyo)
contacts.note: Verify current number before relying on it. After-hours emergencies for Canadians: Emergency Watch and Response Centre, Ottawa +1-613-996-8885 (call collect).

contacts.label: Lost / stolen residence card (在留カード)
contacts.detail: Report to Police (110) for the theft report, then re-apply at Immigration (出入国在留管理庁) within 14 days.
contacts.note: You must carry the card at all times; replacing it needs the police report number.

carry: 在留カード (residence card) — carry it ALWAYS; it is the law and your legal photo ID in Japan.
Health insurance card (国民健康保険証, NHI) once you have enrolled at the ward office.
Passport (or a copy) when the residence card is not yet issued.
A note (paper + phone) with your home address in Japanese, your blood type, and any allergies or medications.
A small amount of cash — many clinics, koban (police boxes), and small shops are cash-only.

restaurants.name: Gyudon chains (Yoshinoya / Sukiya / Matsuya)
restaurants.detail: Beef-bowl chains are the backbone of cheap daily eating. Regular gyudon runs ~¥400-600 (small ~¥400, large ~¥600). Matsuya uses a ticket vending machine at the door; Sukiya/Yoshinoya often have table or counter ordering. Open ~24h at many branches, so they cover late nights after gigs/arcades. Curry, breakfast sets, and miso soup combos keep a full meal under ¥700.
restaurants.how_or_when: No reservation. Walk in anytime. At Matsuya buy a meal ticket from the machine first (English available); hand stub to staff. IC card (Suica/PASMO) accepted at most.

restaurants.name: Standing soba/udon at train stations (tachigui)
restaurants.detail: Stand-and-slurp soba counters inside or beside stations serve a hot bowl in ~90 seconds for ¥300-500. Kake soba (plain) is cheapest; add kakiage (fritter) or tempura for ~¥150 more. A genuinely good budget meal and a daily-commute staple. Notable: Fuji Soba (chain, many 24h), plus platform stands on JR lines.
restaurants.how_or_when: No reservation, no seats. Buy ticket from vending machine, hand to counter, eat standing within minutes. Peak commuter rushes (8am, 6-7pm) get crowded but move fast.
restaurants.area_or_park: Inside/adjacent to most JR and metro stations

restaurants.name: Conveyor-belt sushi (Sushiro / Kura / Hama)
restaurants.detail: Kaiten-zushi chains start around ¥120-150 per plate (prices rose past the old ¥100 line). Most diners eat 8-15 plates for roughly ¥800-1,800. Touchscreen ordering (English/multilingual) sends plates directly to your seat; Kura Sushi gamifies it with a capsule-toy prize every 5 plates. Great for solo software-work breaks.
restaurants.how_or_when: Weekends/dinner: book a slot via the chain's own app (Sushiro app, EPARK for Kura) to skip 60-90 min waits; otherwise take a numbered ticket from the lobby kiosk and wait for your number on screen. Weekday lunch is usually walk-in.
restaurants.area_or_park: Citywide; large branches in Shibuya, Ikebukuro, suburbs

restaurants.name: Saizeriya (cheap Italian-style family chain)
restaurants.detail: Beloved budget Italian: pasta, doria, pizza, and the famous ~¥300 free-refill drink bar. Many dishes ¥300-500; a full plate-plus-drink lunch lands under ¥800. Comfortable booths, free Wi-Fi at many branches, and a tolerant attitude to lingering make it a low-key remote-work or study spot. Order via the paper checklist or table tablet.
restaurants.how_or_when: No reservation. Walk in; for groups put your name on the lobby list. Avoid 12-1pm weekday rush if you want a quiet work seat.

restaurants.name: Supermarket / depachika half-price (han-gaku) bento & sushi
restaurants.detail: The single best WHV money-saver. Supermarkets (Life, Ito-Yokado, Maruetsu, Seiyu) and department-store food halls slap yellow/red discount stickers on prepared bento, sushi, sashimi, and deli items as closing approaches. Discounts typically start ~5pm and deepen to 30-50% off after 8pm. A ¥600 sushi tray becomes ¥300. Stack with point cards.
restaurants.how_or_when: No booking. Arrive 7:30-9pm near closing; look for 半額 (half) / 〇〇円引き (yen-off) stickers. Best selection mid-evening, deepest cuts just before close.
restaurants.area_or_park: Local supermarkets near your apartment; depachika at Shinjuku/Ikebukuro stations

restaurants.name: Izakaya (after-work drinking & small plates)
restaurants.detail: The core social-dining format and your best path for meetups/conventions afterparties. Order rounds of small plates (yakitori, karaage, sashimi, edamame) plus drinks; budget ~¥3,000-4,500/person with a couple of drinks. Watch for the otoshi (compulsory ~¥300-500 seating snack/cover charge) added automatically. Chains (Torikizoku ~¥390/item, Watami) are cheap and reliable; independent izakaya in Shinjuku Omoide Yokocho or Ebisu are atmospheric.
restaurants.how_or_when: Walk-in for chains; for Friday nights or groups 4+, book via Tabelog or the izakaya's own line. As a WHV holder you'll have a Japanese phone + residence card within weeks — call or use the JAPANESE Tabelog flow to dodge the foreigner credit-card requirement. Nomihodai (all-you-can-drink, ~90-120 min) packages must usually be reserved ahead.

restaurants.name: Must-try ramen: Fuunji, Rokurinsha, Nakiryu, Afuri, Ichiran
restaurants.detail: A ramen tour worth doing: Fuunji (Shinjuku) — intense fish-pork tsukemen, arguably the city's best dipping ramen; Rokurinsha (Tokyo Station Ramen Street) — legendary tsukemen, lunch lines of salarymen; Nakiryu (Otsuka) — Michelin-starred tan-tan-men & tsukemen; Afuri (Ebisu/Harajuku) — light yuzu-shio, great entry bowl; Ichiran (Shibuya etc.) — solo tonkotsu booths, fully customizable, tourist-famous but fun once. Bowls ¥1,000-1,600.
restaurants.how_or_when: No reservations anywhere — it's queue culture. Buy a meal ticket from the vending machine at the door, then wait. Go off-peak (right at open ~11am, or 2-3pm) to cut 30-60 min lines at Fuunji/Rokurinsha/Nakiryu. Cash often needed at smaller shops.
restaurants.area_or_park: Shinjuku, Tokyo Station, Otsuka, Ebisu/Harajuku, Shibuya

restaurants.name: Pokémon Cafe (Nihonbashi)
restaurants.detail: Reopened June 17, 2026 after renovation, with strict advance-only web reservations — no day-of walk-ins. Character-themed plates and drinks; the appeal is the experience and exclusive merch, not the food value (expect ~¥2,000-3,500/person plus souvenir cup add-ons). Weekend slots vanish within minutes of release.
restaurants.how_or_when: Book on the official site exactly 31 days in advance at 12:00 (noon) JST — set an alarm and be logged in early. Window noted as opening one month ahead; weekend slots fill in minutes. Verify the exact release time on the official page before your target date.

restaurants.name: Square Enix Cafe (Akihabara) & maid cafes
restaurants.detail: Square Enix Cafe in Akihabara rotates Final Fantasy / Kingdom Hearts / Dragon Quest themed menus and merch in seasonal runs, using advance web booking like Pokémon Cafe — directly on-theme for your games/anime interests. Separately, Akihabara maid cafes (Maidreamin, @home cafe) are walk-in entertainment with a per-hour cover charge plus per-item pricing; treat as a novelty experience, not a meal.
restaurants.how_or_when: Square Enix Cafe: reserve online in advance when a new title-season opens (check official Square Enix Cafe page for the booking window). Maid cafes: walk in; expect a time-limited seating + cover charge and photo fees.
restaurants.price_or_cost: Square Enix ~¥1,500-3,000 + merch; maid cafes cover ~¥700-1,000 + items

restaurants.name: Omakase / sushi splurge — booking via OMAKASE, Pocket Concierge, TableCheck
restaurants.detail: Three English platforms are the foreigner-friendly path to counter omakase and Michelin tables, all accepting foreign Visa/Mastercard. OMAKASE (omakase.in) — counter sushi/kaiseki/tempura/yakiniku, official Michelin Guide partner; ~¥390/seat booking fee plus FULL course prepayment. Pocket Concierge (Amex-owned) — high-end, prepay the full course at booking; narrower list. TableCheck — broadest, usually just a credit-card hold (~¥200) rather than full prepay. Expect course prices ¥15,000-40,000+ at top sushi-ya.
restaurants.how_or_when: Book popular spots 2-4 weeks ahead; Michelin-starred 2-3 months ahead, the moment the booking calendar opens. Create accounts now and watch release dates. Prepaid bookings are non-refundable inside the cancellation window — diarize it.

restaurants.name: The foreigner-reservation problem & your WHV workaround
restaurants.detail: Many top restaurants got burned by foreigner no-shows, so English bookings now demand a credit card and some shops won't take overseas reservations at all (locals/concierge only). Key 2026 change: Tabelog launched a multilingual reservation app (Nov 17, 2025) where credit-card registration is MANDATORY in the English flow — but the JAPANESE-language flow often lets you book with no card. YOUR ADVANTAGE: as a WHV resident you'll get a Japanese phone number and residence card within weeks, so you can use the Japanese Tabelog/phone path and the friendly walk-in treatment locals get. For phone-only shops, options: ask a hotel/host concierge to call, or use a paid agent (TABLEALL ~¥8,000/seat). Always honor or cancel early — no-shows are why this friction exists.
restaurants.how_or_when: Set up a Japanese SIM/number and learn the Japanese Tabelog flow early in your stay. For phone-only Michelin spots, use a concierge or TABLEALL. Cancel well before the cutoff to avoid fees and protect your standing.
restaurants.price_or_cost: Free via Japanese flow / phone; paid agent ~¥8,000/seat

disney.name: Fantasy Springs entry: no area gate, but rides need Standby Pass (free) OR DPA (paid)
disney.detail: Fantasy Springs (DisneySea's newest land: Frozen Kingdom, Rapunzel's Lantern Festival, Peter Pan's Never Land Adventure) currently has NO area-entry restriction. The app shows 'You can enter at any time' when no cap is in place. BUT to ride any of the 3 rides you MUST hold either a free Standby Pass (timed, app-issued, then you queue) or paid Disney Premier Access. The old virtual-queue/Entry Request lottery system ended. Capacity caps can return on peak days, so check the Park Ticket screen in the app on arrival. Strategy: pull a Standby Pass the moment you tap in at the gate, then ride others via DPA or repeat Standby Passes.
disney.how_or_when: In the Tokyo Disney Resort app: 'Standby Pass' and 'Disney Premier Access' tabs. New Standby Pass available 2 hours after your last; new DPA available 1 hour after your last. You can hold one of each simultaneously. Pull-to-refresh through the day as more slots release.
disney.price_or_cost: Standby Pass free; DPA paid (see DPA item)

disney.name: Disney Premier Access (DPA) — paid skip-the-line, dynamic per-ride pricing
disney.detail: DPA is per-person, per-attraction, bought in-app on the day. Dynamic pricing: ¥1,500–¥2,500 depending on ride and crowd. DisneySea: Frozen Kingdom / Rapunzel / Peter Pan (Fantasy Springs) ¥2,000–2,500; Soaring, Toy Story Mania, Tower of Terror ¥1,500–2,000; Journey to the Center of the Earth ~¥1,500; premium shows (Believe! Sea of Dreams, Dance the Globe) up to ¥2,500. Disneyland: Beauty and the Beast ¥1,500–2,500; Baymax, Big Thunder Mountain, Pooh's Hunny Hunt ¥1,500–2,000; Buzz Lightyear ~¥1,500. Space Mountain unavailable (closed for the rebuild). Sources differ on whether the second-DPA gap is a strict 1 hour or 'after you scan' — treat 1 hour as the safe assumption.
disney.how_or_when: Buy in the app on the day. Buy a new DPA 1 hour after the previous one (or after scanning, per some sources). A QR code generates instantly; scan at the Premier Access entrance in your time window. For a synth/games-focused solo trip, spend DPA only on the 3 Fantasy Springs headliners + Soaring; use free Standby Pass / early rope-drop for the rest.

disney.name: 1-Day Passport pricing — date-specific, variable (weekday vs weekend/peak)
disney.detail: Adult 1-Day 1-Park Passport runs roughly ¥7,900 (cheapest weekdays) to ¥10,900 (peak weekends/holidays). Junior ~¥6,600–9,000, Child ~¥4,700–5,600. Price is tied to the specific date you choose — Tokyo Disney does NOT do open-dated gate tickets and does NOT sell at the gate. Moving a Saturday plan to a Tue/Wed midweek date saves ¥2,000–3,000 per adult AND gives shorter lines. As a 12-month WHV resident you have huge flexibility — always pick a cheap-tier weekday.
disney.how_or_when: Tier/price for a given date appears on the official ticket calendar ~2 months out. Pick lowest-tier midweek dates outside school breaks.

disney.name: Online ticket buying window — open ~2 months (60 days) ahead, date-specific
disney.detail: Fixed-date tickets release for purchase about 2 months (60 days) in advance on the official site/app. Booking exactly at the 60-day mark at 2:00 PM Japan time gives the widest selection; weekends and event days can sell out. Buy via: official Tokyo Disney Resort website/app (needs a MyDisney account — tickets auto-link), Klook (QR code you scan into the app), or — once in Japan — 7-Eleven/Lawson/FamilyMart convenience-store machines. No gate sales.
disney.how_or_when: Set a reminder for ~60 days before your target date; buy at 14:00 JST. Link to the app immediately so the QR lives on your phone.

disney.name: Tokyo Disney Resort app — mandatory toolkit, set up BEFORE the gate
disney.detail: The app is non-negotiable: it holds your ticket QR (scanned at the gate), real-time wait times, show schedules, park map, restaurant menus + mobile dining orders, Standby Pass, DPA, and the free Entry Request lottery for parades/shows/character greetings. Download and link all tickets before you fly; create a MyDisney account. CRITICAL gotcha: you need working mobile data inside the park or the app is dead weight — as a WHV resident, get a Japanese SIM/eSIM early (don't rely on tourist data). The first 30 minutes after opening are when Standby Passes/DPA move fastest, so be logged in and ready at the gate.
disney.how_or_when: Set up the night before. App Store / Google Play 'Tokyo Disney Resort App'. Have data + battery bank.

disney.name: Cheapest + least-crowded days — Tue/Wed/Thu, avoid peaks
disney.detail: Best value AND shortest lines: Tuesday–Thursday, outside school breaks. Saturday and Friday are busiest; Monday is a busier weekday. Quietest windows in your 12 months: mid-Sept to early Nov (after Obon, before Christmas season) and late Jan to mid-Feb (after New Year, before spring break). June (your arrival month) and July are also relatively quiet. AVOID: Golden Week (late Apr–early May, an extreme global peak), Obon (mid-Aug), Christmas/New Year (Dec 27–Jan 3), and Feb-late/March spring breaks. A cheap-tier weekday stacks the savings (lower ticket price) with the experience (lower waits = less DPA spend needed).
disney.how_or_when: Target Tue/Wed/Thu in your first weeks (early–mid July is quiet) and again Sept–Oct. Cross-check a crowd calendar (queue-times.com, thrill-data.com, TDR Explorer) before booking.
disney.price_or_cost: Saves ¥2,000–3,000/ticket vs weekend + less DPA needed

disney.name: DisneySea 25th Anniversary 'Sparkling Jubilee' — the headline event of your whole stay
disney.detail: Runs Apr 15, 2026 – Mar 31, 2027, so it covers essentially your ENTIRE 12-month window (you land June 30, 2026). Signature color 'Jubilee Blue', anniversary décor, special merch, and new shows. Tie-ins to plan around: 'Dance the Globe' (debuts Jan 14, 2026) — 25-min music/dance show at Waterfront Park, 2–4x/day; 'D-Groovationz4 Live' concert-style show debuts Sept 30, 2026 at Showbase, Tomorrowland (strong fit for your music interest); plus the DisneySea Food & Wine Festival ('travel the world through cuisine'). Anniversary merch and limited shows sell out — go early in your stay and revisit, since you have the whole year.
disney.how_or_when: Apr 15, 2026–Mar 31, 2027. Hit it on a quiet weekday; book anniversary shows via Entry Request lottery or DPA in-app on the day.
disney.price_or_cost: Included with park admission; premium shows DPA ¥2,500

disney.name: Disney Halloween 2026 — Sept 16–Oct 31
disney.detail: Runs Sept 16 – Oct 31, 2026 at both parks. Special parades/shows, seasonal food, merch, and full-costume days for guests on designated dates. Falls in one of the year's quieter, cheaper windows (mid-Sept to early Nov), so it's a sweet spot: festive atmosphere without peak prices or peak crowds. Great early target since you'll be settled in Tokyo by then.
disney.how_or_when: Sept 16–Oct 31, 2026. Pick a Tue/Wed/Thu. Included with admission. Dates are official/annual-pattern but confirm exact 2026 dates on the official events page closer.

disney.name: Disney Christmas 2026 — Nov 11–Dec 25
disney.detail: Runs Nov 11 – Dec 25, 2026 at both parks: Christmas trees, illuminations, seasonal parades/shows, food and merch. Beautiful but November is one of the most popular months and December gets crowded as it builds toward the holidays — pricing tiers run high. Go on a weekday early in the window (mid-Nov) rather than the Dec 27–Jan 3 New Year crush. DisneySea's harbor illuminations pair with the 25th Anniversary décor this year.
disney.how_or_when: Nov 11–Dec 25, 2026. Weekday in mid-to-late Nov is the value pick; avoid the last 10 days before Christmas. Included with admission.
disney.price_or_cost: Included; expect high ticket tiers on these dates

disney.name: Vacation Packages — the only way to GUARANTEE Fantasy Springs rides (skip the pass scramble)
disney.detail: A Tokyo Disney Resort Vacation Package bundles an on-site hotel + dated park tickets + perks. The killer perk: the 2-day 'Enjoy Attractions Even More' plan includes a Fantasy Springs Magic pass giving unlimited Fantasy Springs area access AND Premier Access to all 3 Fantasy Springs rides — no Standby Pass scramble, no per-ride DPA fees. Also includes selectable hotel (Fantasy Springs Hotel, MiraCosta, Disneyland Hotel, Ambassador, Toy Story Hotel), popcorn bucket voucher, original-goods voucher, beverage ticket, and pre-bookable character-greeting times. Expensive vs a day ticket, but if you want guaranteed Fantasy Springs without app stress, this is the lever. As a year-long resident you can splurge once and DIY the rest.
disney.how_or_when: Book in advance via the official Vacation Package site (reserve.tokyodisneyresort.jp). Packages open further ahead than day tickets and sell out — book well in advance (think months, especially for Fantasy Springs Hotel). Verify current package tiers/pricing closer.
disney.price_or_cost: Splurge — hotel + tickets + perks bundle (varies by hotel/date)

disney.name: Disney Hotel 'Happy Entry' — early-entry edge for Fantasy Springs
disney.detail: Guests staying at the four Tokyo Disney Resort Disney Hotels (incl. Fantasy Springs Hotel, MiraCosta, Disneyland Hotel, Ambassador) get 'Happy Entry' — admission ~15 minutes before official opening on their checkout day at the corresponding park. With Fantasy Springs now on a first-come Standby Pass system, that head start materially improves your odds of grabbing early Standby Passes / riding before the crowd. Fantasy Springs Hotel guests get the strongest Fantasy Springs access. A one-night on-site stay is a tactical splurge to nail the hard-to-ride headliners.
disney.how_or_when: Stay at a Disney Hotel; Happy Entry applies on checkout day at the matching park. Confirm exact lead-time minutes and eligible park on the official page when booking.

disney.name: Free Entry Request lottery — parades, shows & character greetings
disney.detail: Entry Request is a FREE in-app lottery for designated parades, shows, and character greetings (separate from paid DPA). You request a slot; if you win, you get an assigned viewing area/time. For the 25th Anniversary shows and seasonal Halloween/Christmas entertainment, this is how you secure good spots without paying. Budget-friendly counterpart to DPA — use it aggressively. Note it's a lottery, not guaranteed, so have a backup plan.
disney.how_or_when: In the app's 'Entry Request' section once you're in the park. Request early in the day; results are instant. Free.

activities.name: Ghibli Museum (Mitaka) — lottery-style monthly ticket drop
activities.detail: Intimate Miyazaki/Studio Ghibli museum in Mitaka, west Tokyo (~25 min from Shinjuku). Date- and time-slot-specific, no same-day tickets, sells out within minutes. Strict no-photos inside; exclusive short film in the Saturn Theater. A must for the anime/tech-culture interest, and a quick half-day rather than a full day trip.
activities.how_or_when: Tickets via Lawson Ticket only. Released 10:00 JST on the 10th of each month for the FOLLOWING month (e.g. buy Aug 10 for Sep). Foreigners can use the English Lawson page; ticket carries the lead visitor's passport name and ID is checked at entry. Set a phone alarm for 09:55 JST on the 10th and be logged in early. Four entry slots: 10:00 / 12:00 / 14:00 / 16:00.
activities.price_or_cost: Adult ¥1,000 (cheap; the scarcity is the cost, not the price)

activities.name: Ghibli Park (Aichi) — advance-reservation overnight trip
activities.detail: Full theme park near Nagoya (Aichi Expo site): Grand Warehouse, Valley of Witches (Howl's castle, Kiki's bakery), Mononoke Village, Dondoko Forest. Bigger and newer than the Mitaka museum. ~1h40 by Shinkansen Tokyo→Nagoya then ~30–40 min to Aichikyuhaku-kinen-koen; realistically an overnight, not a Tokyo day trip. The Grand Warehouse slot is the hardest to get.
activities.how_or_when: All tickets are advance reservation, no door sales. Official release 14:00 JST on the 10th of the month, TWO months ahead (e.g. tickets for August go on sale June 10). Lawson/official channel needs a Japanese address + Japanese card; as a WHV holder living in Tokyo you'll have both, OR use Klook/KKday/JTB which support English/foreign cards. Avoid the 27th (Aichi-resident priority cuts general stock). Verify exact monthly times closer.
activities.price_or_cost: Area tickets roughly ¥1,500–¥7,800 depending on areas/Grand Warehouse; plus ~¥11,000+ Shinkansen each way and a hotel night

activities.name: Grand Sumo — Ryogoku Kokugikan (Tokyo basho)
activities.detail: Watching a 15-day tournament (basho) at Ryogoku Kokugikan is a signature Tokyo experience. Three of the six annual basho are in Tokyo. Go early afternoon for atmosphere, or arrive ~15:00 for the top-division (makuuchi) bouts and the closing ring-entering ceremony. Cheap upper-deck chair seats vs splurge ringside masu boxes.
activities.how_or_when: 2026 Tokyo dates: Hatsu (January) basho Jan 11–25, 2026; Aki (September) basho Sep 13–27, 2026; the May (Natsu) basho also runs in Tokyo. Tickets sell fast — Jan tickets went on sale Dec 6, 2025; Sep tickets on sale Aug 8, 2026 (the May basho tickets typically open early/mid April). Buy via the official Ticket Oosumo site or buysumotickets.com. Verify the May 2026 dates closer.
activities.price_or_cost: Single upper chair seats from ~¥3,000–¥8,500; masu box seats ¥10,000–¥20,000+ per person

activities.name: teamLab Borderless (Azabudai Hills) — permanent
activities.detail: Immersive digital-art museum, the permanent successor to the old Odaiba Borderless. Wandering, boundaryless rooms where artworks move between spaces. Central location in the new Azabudai Hills complex. Strong fit for the tech-culture interest. Timed entry; reflective/light-colored clothing recommended for the mirror rooms.
activities.how_or_when: Advance timed-entry only, no on-site sales. Book on the official teamLab site, Klook or GetYourGuide ~2–3 weeks ahead for weekends/sunset slots. Open year-round.

activities.name: teamLab Planets (Toyosu) — go before it closes
activities.detail: The barefoot, wade-through-water teamLab venue in Toyosu — different from Borderless (water rooms, the floating-orchid garden, mirrored infinity spaces). Roll up your pants; you walk through knee-deep water. Currently slated to close at the END OF 2027, so prioritize it during your WHV year while it's still open.
activities.how_or_when: Advance timed tickets via official DMM store or Klook, book ~2–3 weeks ahead. Closure currently extended to end of 2027 — verify the closing date closer, as it has been pushed back before.

activities.name: Hakone loop — Mt Fuji, Owakudani & onsen (Hakone Free Pass)
activities.detail: The classic Fuji-view day trip / overnight: mountain railway to Gora, cablecar + ropeway over the sulfur vents of Owakudani (eat a black egg), pirate-ship cruise on Lake Ashi with Fuji behind it, the Hakone Open-Air Museum, and onsen at Hakone-Yumoto. Doable in a long day but better as a one-night ryokan stay. Clearest Fuji views in winter (Dec–Feb).
activities.how_or_when: Buy the Odakyu Hakone Free Pass from Shinjuku (covers round-trip + all 8 Hakone transport modes + discounts). Add the Romancecar limited express for a reserved, faster ride. Buy online via Odakyu's EMot/global site or at the Shinjuku ticket counter. Any season; go on a clear day for Fuji.
activities.price_or_cost: Hakone Free Pass ~¥6,100 (2-day from Shinjuku); Romancecar +~¥1,200 each way

activities.name: Nikko — Toshogu shrine & Kegon Falls (UNESCO)
activities.detail: Ornate, gold-leafed Toshogu Shrine complex (Tokugawa Ieyasu's mausoleum, the 'see/hear/speak no evil' monkeys, Yomeimon Gate) plus the dramatic Kegon Falls and Lake Chuzenji up the Irohazaka switchbacks. Big, full day. Spectacular autumn foliage (late Oct–early Nov). Premium SPACIA X limited express makes the ride part of the fun.
activities.how_or_when: Tobu Railway from Asakusa (~2h on SPACIA X / Revaty Kegon). Get the Tobu 'Nikko Pass All Area' (~¥4,780) if you want Kegon Falls + Lake Chuzenji buses; the cheaper 'World Heritage Area' pass (~¥2,000) covers just the shrines. Leave Asakusa 7–8am. Reserve limited-express seats in advance for foliage season.

activities.name: Kamakura — Great Buddha, Enoden coast & bamboo temple
activities.detail: Seaside ancient-capital day trip: the bronze Great Buddha (Daibutsu) at Kotoku-in, Hasedera's gardens and sea views, Hokokuji's serene bamboo grove with matcha, Tsurugaoka Hachimangu shrine, snack-hunting on Komachi-dori, and the nostalgic Enoden train hugging the coast toward Enoshima. Easy, cheap, no advance booking needed.
activities.how_or_when: JR Yokosuka Line from Tokyo/Shinjuku Station (~60 min, ~¥920). Take the Enoden 3 stops to Hase for the Buddha. Consider the Enoden 1-day 'Noriorikun' pass if hopping stops. Year-round; hydrangeas at Hasedera in June, autumn colors at Hokokuji.
activities.price_or_cost: ~¥3,000–4,000 total day (JR return + Great Buddha ¥300 + Hasedera + konbini lunch)

activities.name: Kawagoe — 'Little Edo' half-day
activities.detail: Edo-era warehouse (kurazukuri) streets, the Toki no Kane bell tower, Kashiya Yokocho candy alley and sweet-potato treats — a compact, low-cost retro-Japan half-day that pairs well with renting a kimono. Closer and quicker than most day trips, good for a relaxed weekend morning.
activities.how_or_when: Tobu Tojo Line from Ikebukuro (~30 min express) or Seibu Shinjuku Line. Optional Tobu 'Kawagoe Discount Pass' bundles the train + local bus loop. No reservations needed; go on a weekday to dodge crowds.
activities.price_or_cost: ~¥1,000–1,500 round-trip; attractions mostly free or a few hundred yen

activities.name: Mt Takao — easy half-day hike with cablecar
activities.detail: Tokyo's favorite mountain, ~1 hour from Shinjuku. Cablecar or chairlift takes you halfway, then Trail 1 (~50 min, paved/gentle) leads past Yakuoin temple to the 599m summit with Fuji views on clear days. Great low-effort nature break, soba and tengu lore at the top. Autumn foliage and New-Year sunrise are peak draws (and peak crowds).
activities.how_or_when: Keio Line semi-limited express from Shinjuku to Takaosanguchi (~50 min). The Keio 'Takao 1-Day Ticket' (~¥1,690) covers the round-trip + cablecar/chairlift. No booking needed. Go early on clear autumn days; avoid late afternoon last-cablecar rush.
activities.price_or_cost: ~¥1,690 Keio 1-day ticket (incl. cablecar); train-only ~¥430 each way

activities.name: Street go-kart through Tokyo (Akihabara/Shibuya) — bring your Canadian IDP
activities.detail: Costumed go-karts driving real Tokyo streets past Tokyo Station, Ginza, Shibuya Scramble or the Rainbow Bridge — a bucket-list novelty fitting the games/arcade vibe. CRITICAL Canadian gotcha: you need a valid driving license PLUS an International Driving Permit. Canada is a 1949 Geneva Convention country, so a Canadian IDP works directly — but the IDP CANNOT be issued in Japan, so get it from CAA in Canada BEFORE you fly (it's valid 1 year). Note: a Japanese-issued license also works once you have one, but the IDP is the simplest route. No original docs at the shop = no drive, no refund.
activities.how_or_when: Book Street Kart (kart.st) or similar via their site, Klook or Viator a few days ahead. Bring your physical Canadian license + physical IDP. Tours run day and night; night runs through Shibuya/Tokyo Tower are the photogenic pick.
activities.price_or_cost: ~¥7,000–10,000 for ~1–2 hours; costume rental usually included

activities.name: Observation decks — Shibuya Sky, Tokyo Skytree, Tokyo City View
activities.detail: Three signature city-view spots. Shibuya Sky: open-air rooftop at 229m, no glass overhead, best ~30 min before sunset over the Scramble. Tokyo Skytree: Japan's tallest at 634m, Tembo Deck (350m) and Tembo Galleria (450m) with glass floors. Tokyo City View (Mori Tower, Roppongi, 52F): best photogenic angle on Tokyo Tower and, on clear winter days, Fuji — often bundled with the Mori Art Museum.
activities.how_or_when: Shibuya Sky: book a timed slot online (¥2,200 online vs ¥2,500 door); sunset slots sell out — book days ahead. Skytree: pre-book to skip lines, open 10:00–21:00. Mori Tower City View: usually walk-up. Clear winter afternoons give the best Fuji odds.
activities.price_or_cost: Shibuya Sky ¥2,200 online; Skytree ~¥2,100–3,400; Mori Tower City View ~¥2,000 (often incl. Mori Art Museum)

activities.name: Onsen-town escapes — Atami, Hakone-Yumoto, Kusatsu
activities.detail: For seasonal hot-spring trips: Atami is the fastest (seaside retro resort, ~40 min by Shinkansen from Tokyo Station) and great as a day trip; Hakone-Yumoto pairs onsen with the Fuji loop; Kusatsu (Japan's premier sulfur onsen, the steaming yubatake field) is ~2.5h by JR bus and best as an overnight, especially in winter snow. Many ryokan offer day-use (higaeri) bathing if you don't stay over.
activities.how_or_when: Atami: Tokaido Shinkansen Kodama/Hikari from Tokyo. Hakone-Yumoto: Odakyu Romancecar from Shinjuku (~1h25). Kusatsu: JR bus from Tokyo/Shinjuku, reserve ahead. Best in autumn (Oct–Nov foliage) and winter; outdoor (rotenburo) baths shine in the cold. Tattoos can be an issue at some baths — check policy or pick tattoo-friendly spots.
activities.price_or_cost: Day-use onsen ~¥1,000–2,500; transport ¥2,000–6,000 each way; ryokan overnight ¥12,000–30,000+ pp with meals

activities.name: Tokyo Disney Resort (DisneySea / Disneyland) — date-based tickets & app
activities.detail: DisneySea (the more adult/unique park, with the new Fantasy Springs Frozen/Tangled/Peter Pan area) and Disneyland.
