Date-specific dated tickets with dynamic pricing; weekday off-peak is cheaper and far less crowded. Use the official Tokyo Disney Resort app on the day for Standby Pass, Priority Pass and paid Premier Access to skip the worst queues. A full day each; go on a weekday given your year-long flexibility.
activities.how_or_when: JR Keiyo/Musashino Line to Maihama (~15–20 min from Tokyo Station). Buy dated tickets in advance on the official site/app — popular dates and Fantasy Springs sell out. Prices vary by date; cheapest on quiet winter weekdays.

building.name: WeWork All Access Plus (membership) + WeWork day pass (drop-in)
building.detail: The flagship gaijin-friendly option with the densest Tokyo network (40+ buildings: Shinjuku, Shibuya, Roppongi, Marunouchi, Iidabashi, etc.). All Access Plus lets you hot-desk at ANY WeWork in Japan (and worldwide) — ideal if you move around the city or travel within Japan. English-speaking community team, reliable fast wifi, free coffee/tea, printing, phone booths for your Canada calls, and frequent member networking events. For occasional use, buy single 'WeWork Day Pass' bookings via the app instead of committing monthly.
building.how_or_when: Sign up at wework.co.jp/en. Book day passes or tour via the WeWork app. No Japanese needed. Bring residence card / passport for first visit.
building.area_or_park: Network across Tokyo (Shinjuku, Shibuya, Roppongi, Marunouchi, Iidabashi, Ginza)
building.price_or_cost: All Access Plus ~JPY 42,900/mo tax incl.; single day passes typically ~JPY 3,000-5,000 booked in-app (verify current rate)

building.name: fabbit (drop-in friendly coworking chain)
building.detail: Budget-friendly Japanese coworking chain that genuinely welcomes drop-ins — the best 'just need a desk today' option. Locations include Kyobashi, Otemachi, and others. Quiet, clean, reliable wifi and power, good for heads-down dev work. Less of an international scene than WeWork but far cheaper for casual use. Good fallback when you don't want a monthly commitment.
building.how_or_when: Walk in or reserve via fabbit site. Some locations English-light, so use translation app. Best for occasional desk days.
building.area_or_park: Kyobashi, Otemachi and other central Tokyo nodes
building.price_or_cost: One-day pass around JPY 2,200/day at Kyobashi (verify per location); monthly plans available

building.name: MIDORI.so (Nakameguro / Ikejiri / Nagatacho / Bakuroyokoyama)
building.detail: The creative/design-leaning community space — ivy-covered renovated buildings, art gallery, cafe, weekly community lunches and workshops. Strong international, creative-professional crowd (designers, founders, indie makers), which fits your music-gear/anime/maker interests better than a corporate tower. More 'community' than 'office'. Pay-as-you-go option makes it easy to trial before committing.
building.how_or_when: Tour/apply at midori.so/en. English-friendly. Start with a pay-as-you-go day to feel out the community.
building.price_or_cost: Pay-as-you-go ~JPY 3,300/day or JPY 33,000 for an 11-day pack; Basic membership ~JPY 29,700/mo; full from ~JPY 42,350/mo; private booths from ~JPY 71,500/mo + JPY 11,000 admission

building.name: CIC Tokyo (Toranomon Hills Business Tower, 15-16F)
building.detail: Japan's largest innovation center and the single best place to plug into the Tokyo startup/dev ecosystem — ~280 startups, VCs, mentors, and corporates under one roof. This is where Venture Cafe's weekly 'Thursday Gathering' (free community networking) and many tech/startup events happen. Even if you don't take a desk, you can attend events here. Treat it as your community anchor, not just an office. Full coworking memberships are pricey/team-oriented; the value for a solo WHV dev is the events and network.
building.how_or_when: For events: register free via Venture Cafe Tokyo (venturecafeglobal.org) Thursday Gatherings. For desk/office: contact jp.cic.com/en. Go for the network even on a budget.
building.price_or_cost: Coworking/office is enterprise-priced (private rooms from ~JPY 200,000/mo); BUT Venture Cafe Thursday Gathering events are typically FREE to attend

building.name: Tokyo Metropolitan Central Library (Hiroo / Arisugawa Park)
building.detail: The single best FREE work spot in Tokyo for focused remote work. Free fast wifi (~140 Mbps, no time limit), ~300 seats WITH power outlets (~600 more without), huge English-language section, quiet, reservable individual research desks and a 5F group study room, plus an affordable cafeteria with a park view. Sits in leafy Arisugawa Park. Perfect for deep-focus dev days and Canada-overlap evening calls don't apply here (closes evening) but unbeatable for daytime cost of zero.
building.how_or_when: Just walk in during opening hours (closes evenings, some Mondays/holidays — verify on library.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/english). Arrive early on weekends for power seats.
building.price_or_cost: Free (no membership needed to enter and use; bring ID). Cafeteria meals cheap.

building.name: Musashino Place (free modern library/work space, Musashisakai)
building.detail: If you settle west/central Tokyo, this is a standout free public 'third place' — light-filled modern architecture, free wifi, plentiful power outlets, study/co-working zones, and a small cafe. Far more pleasant and design-forward than a typical library. Great free alternative to paid coworking on days you want quiet ambience without spending.
building.how_or_when: Walk in during opening hours (verify schedule). Good base if you live along the Chuo line.
building.price_or_cost: Free to use; cafe drinks cheap

building.name: Work-friendly cafe shortlist: Caffice, FabCafe, LATTEST, The Bridge, kissaten Renoir
building.detail: For ad-hoc work between meetings: Caffice (Shinjuku) is explicitly a 'cafe-meets-office' with free wifi and laptop-friendly seating; FabCafe (Shibuya/Hibiya) is a maker/tech cafe with abundant outlets and free wifi (fits your hardware/synth interest); LATTEST (Omotesando) has USB charging at every table; The Bridge (above Harajuku Station) has good wifi and plugs; kissaten Renoir (many central locations, e.g. Ginza) is a retro chain where you can camp for hours undisturbed with outlets. Note many indie Tokyo cafes discourage long laptop sessions and some lack outlets — these specific ones are safe bets.
building.how_or_when: Drop in. Buy a drink, ask/look for outlet seats. Avoid camping at tiny indie kissaten during busy hours. Renoir/Tully's/Doutor chains are the reliable fallback citywide.
building.area_or_park: Shinjuku, Shibuya, Hibiya, Omotesando, Harajuku, Ginza
building.price_or_cost: Cost of a drink (~JPY 500-700); some (Caffice) have time/per-use pricing — check on arrival

building.name: Canada<->Japan timezone reality for async remote work
building.detail: Tokyo is JST (UTC+9, no daylight saving). In summer Tokyo is 13h ahead of Toronto (EDT) and 16h ahead of Vancouver (PDT); in winter it's 14h ahead of Toronto (EST) and 17h ahead of Vancouver (PST). Practical reality: your Tokyo MORNING (e.g., 8-11am JST) overlaps with the previous Canadian afternoon/evening — that's your live-meeting window. A 9am ET Toronto standup = 10pm JST same day; a Vancouver 9am PT = 1am JST. Plan for genuinely async work: do your synchronous calls early-morning or late-evening JST, and keep the rest async (written updates, recorded Looms, clear handoffs). This 'follow-the-sun' offset is actually a selling point — you can deliver work overnight for Canadian colleagues. Block your calendar so you don't get pulled into 11pm-2am JST meetings daily.
building.how_or_when: Anchor live overlap to early-morning JST (overlaps Canada afternoon) or late-evening JST. Use a world-clock tool and protect sleep. Confirm exact offset around DST changeovers (Canada springs forward early/mid-March, falls back early November; Japan has no DST).

building.name: Connectivity #1: Japanese SIM/eSIM after you get your Residence Card (best value)
building.detail: On a 12-month WHV you'll have a Residence Card (zairyu card) — get a proper resident MVNO plan, not a tourist SIM, for the best price. IIJmio (Docomo network) offers ~3-55GB plans from roughly JPY 1,000-1,500/mo and is among the cheapest, but has little English support. Once settled, compare Rakuten Mobile, ahamo, LINEMO, UQ mobile, povo. Most require a Japanese address, Residence Card, and a Japanese bank account or credit card — so set up banking first. This is your primary, cheapest line for the year.
building.how_or_when: Apply after you have Residence Card + Japanese bank/credit card. IIJmio/Rakuten online; carriers in-store. Gotcha: many MVNOs need a Japanese payment card — get a bank account (e.g., Japan Post / Sony Bank / Rakuten) or a card first.
building.price_or_cost: IIJmio from ~JPY 1,000-1,500/mo for several GB; carrier plans (ahamo/LINEMO) ~JPY 2,000-3,000/mo for 20-30GB

building.name: Connectivity #2: English-support bridge SIM (Sakura Mobile / Mobal) for week 1
building.detail: For your first weeks before banking is set up, use an English-support resident SIM so you have a phone number for apartment/bank/utility sign-ups. Sakura Mobile gives full English support (Shinjuku office, airport counters, chat) — ~JPY 3,278/mo for 5GB, ~JPY 4,378/mo for 30GB. Mobal provides a voice+data eSIM WITH a Japanese phone number (essential for verifications) and no Residence Card required to start. More expensive than IIJmio, but the English support + immediate Japanese number removes week-1 friction; switch to a cheaper MVNO once settled.
building.how_or_when: Order before you fly; pick up eSIM/SIM at NRT on arrival 2026-06-30. Use it to get a Japanese number for bank/apartment, then port or switch to IIJmio/carrier later.
building.area_or_park: Airport pickup at NRT / Shinjuku office
building.price_or_cost: Sakura Mobile ~JPY 3,278/mo (5GB) to ~JPY 4,378/mo (30GB); Mobal voice+data plans from ~USD 11.50

building.name: Connectivity #3: Home internet + backup for reliable client calls
building.detail: For video calls with Canada you want wired stability, not just mobile. Many Tokyo apartments come with building fiber you can activate quickly; otherwise a no-contract pocket-wifi or home router (e.g. Rakuten Turbo / WiMAX-type) is a fast stopgap while fixed fiber (NURO/au/SoftBank) is installed — fiber installs can take 2-6 weeks. Keep your phone plan with enough data + tethering as call backup. A wired Ethernet adapter for your laptop noticeably improves call reliability versus shared apartment wifi.
building.how_or_when: Activate building fiber day one if available; order pocket-wifi as interim. Book fiber install early (long lead time). Share houses often include wifi — test its speed/upload before committing to client calls.
building.price_or_cost: Fiber ~JPY 4,000-6,000/mo; pocket-wifi / home router ~JPY 4,000-5,000/mo no-contract; Ethernet adapter ~JPY 1,500 one-time

building.name: Where the dev/startup community gathers (your networking plan)
building.detail: Plug in fast via these regulars: (1) Venture Cafe Tokyo 'Thursday Gathering' at CIC Tokyo/Toranomon Hills — free, weekly, the biggest open innovation mixer. (2) TokyoDev community (Paul McMahon) + Tokyo Rubyist Meetup — friendly, English, dev-focused talks and the best job board for foreigners in Japanese tech. (3) Tokyo Tech Meetup — international, quarterly. (4) ProductTank Tokyo — monthly, product/PM. (5) Le Wagon Tokyo — frequent free talks/workshops. (6) Builders Weekend — 48h hackathon (next edition late Mar-early Apr 2026) great for meeting builders fast. Track everything on dev.events/meetups/AS/JP/Tokyo and Meetup.com. As a solo remote dev, these are how you avoid isolation and find collaborators for your music-gear/games side projects.
building.how_or_when: Register: Venture Cafe Thursday Gathering (venturecafeglobal.org), TokyoDev.com newsletter/community, Meetup.com (Tokyo Tech Meetup, ProductTank Tokyo), dev.events for the full calendar. Start the first month — momentum matters.
building.price_or_cost: Most meetups free or ~JPY 1,000-2,000; hackathons sometimes a small fee

building.name: Co-ba (community-driven coworking network)
building.detail: Co-ba is a long-running Japanese community-coworking brand (Shibuya and other branches) oriented toward freelancers, creators, and small startups rather than corporate tenants — a friendlier, cheaper, more local-community feel than WeWork. Good middle option if WeWork is too pricey/sterile and you want to meet Japanese indie makers and founders. Branch availability and pricing vary, so check the specific location.
building.how_or_when: Tour the Shibuya branch; check current branches and plans on the co-ba site. More Japanese-language than WeWork — translation app helps.
building.price_or_cost: Monthly memberships vary by branch (roughly JPY 16,000-25,000/mo typical for community coworking); verify per location

building.name: WHV remote-work money gotchas (tax, pension, proof of funds)
building.detail: Critical for a Canadian WHV doing remote dev: (1) PROOF OF FUNDS — the WHV application requires showing sufficient funds (commonly cited around CAD 2,500+ plus return airfare, or more without a ticket) — have bank statements ready. (2) TAX — once you're a Japanese tax resident, income (including some remote earnings) can be taxable in Japan; Canada and Japan have a tax treaty to avoid double taxation, but you should confirm your situation with a tax professional and check CRA residency rules (you may become a non-resident or factual resident of Canada). (3) PENSION — if you enrol in Japan's pension system, you may be eligible for a Lump-sum Withdrawal Payment when you leave (claim within 2 years of departure); a Canada-Japan social security agreement lets you coordinate contributions. (4) Working Holiday visa generally allows work to supplement travel — confirm current rules on remote work for a foreign (Canadian) employer with the embassy. Mark all specifics 'verify closer' as rules and thresholds change.
building.how_or_when: Verify proof-of-funds amount on the Japanese Embassy in Canada site before/at application; consult a cross-border tax advisor after arrival; claim pension lump-sum within 2 years of leaving Japan. Verify closer — figures change.
building.price_or_cost: Proof of funds ~CAD 2,500+ (verify current requirement); pension lump-sum refundable on departure; tax varies

music.name: Ochanomizu Guitar Street (Meidai-dori / Gakkitengai)
music.detail: Tokyo's guitar/gear district: ~50 instrument shops clustered along Meidai-dori next to Ochanomizu Station. New, vintage and heavily-discounted used guitars, basses, amps, effects/pedals from every major brand (Fender, Gibson, Gretsch, Rickenbacker, PRS). Shops are packed close so price-comparison is easy. This is your home base for stringed gear and the easiest single afternoon to scope the whole scene.
music.how_or_when: JR Chuo/Sobu Line or Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line to Ochanomizu Stn — street is at the exit. Most shops 11:00-20:00. Weekday afternoons quietest.
music.price_or_cost: Free to browse; used guitars from ~¥10,000 to vintage 6-figures.

music.name: Shimokura / Shimokura Second Hands (Ochanomizu)
music.detail: Anchor chain on Guitar Street. Good first stop on the street for used stringed gear and to gauge market prices before buying elsewhere.
music.how_or_when: Steps from Ochanomizu Stn on Meidai-dori. See access map on their site.

music.name: Miki Gakki (Ochanomizu)
music.detail: Osaka-based national chain with a strong Ochanomizu presence (including a 'Smalls' boutique guitar shop). Wide selection across guitars, amps and effects; part of the Ochanomizu cluster so easy to combine with the rest of the street. Note Miki Gakki also historically ran wind/band-instrument and pro stores — the Ochanomizu locations are guitar-focused.
music.how_or_when: ~4-min walk from JR/Metro Ochanomizu Stn. Typical hours ~11:00-20:00.
music.price_or_cost: Free to browse; new and used stock across price tiers;

music.name: Five G Music Technology (synth mecca)
music.detail: Legendary synth shop near Harajuku/Shibuya — Tokyo's single best destination for vintage AND modern synthesizers: classic analog mono/polysynths, modern keyboard synths, drum machines, plus a well-known Eurorack wall. Major Dave Smith Instruments/Sequential dealer. Staff repair and refurbish vintage units in-house, so vintage stock turnover can be slow and prices are fair-not-bargain. Treat it as a pilgrimage; serious vintage hunters should call/check stock before going.
music.how_or_when: Walk from Harajuku/Meiji-jingumae or Shibuya. Confirm current hours/address via shop before visiting; verify closer to your trip.
music.price_or_cost: Free to browse; used/vintage synths typically ¥50,000-¥500,000+. Not a bargain shop.

music.name: Rock oN Company (Shibuya)
music.detail: Pro audio / music-production specialist (est. 1989) in Jinnan, Shibuya. Best in Tokyo for DAWs, audio interfaces, monitors, controllers, plugins and studio gear — they've shifted from old analog synths toward pro production gear in recent years. Consultative, proposal-based shopping; good for kitting out a home/remote studio. English-friendly enough for browsing.
music.how_or_when: 1-8-18-1F Jinnan, Shibuya-ku. Open ~12:00-19:00, closed Sundays and irregular holidays. ~7-min walk from Shibuya Stn.
music.price_or_cost: Free to browse; gear from entry-level interfaces (~¥15,000) to pro monitors/converters.

music.name: Clockface Modular — 'Hatagaya Patching Center' showroom
music.detail: Japan's leading Eurorack-focused dealer (online since 2014) with a physical showroom you can visit to patch and audition modules before buying. Carries international Eurorack brands plus boutique Japanese makers including Tokyo Tape Music Center (Buchla-inspired modules). The single most useful stop for eurorack/modular in Tokyo. Showroom visits may be appointment-based — email ahead.
music.how_or_when: Rm 1, Ritz Palacio, 8-4-701 Hatagaya, Shibuya-ku (7F). ~6-min walk from Hatagaya Stn (Keio New Line) or Sasazuka Stn (Keio Line). Email/check site for showroom hours/booking; verify closer.
music.price_or_cost: Free to visit/audition; individual modules typically ¥15,000-¥80,000+. A starter rack runs ¥150,000+.

music.name: Tokyo Tape Music Center (Buchla-style modules)
music.detail: Boutique Tokyo maker of Buchla-100-inspired Eurorack modules (Dual Sine/Sawtooth 158, Dual Square 144, Tokyo Gate, etc.) — a play on the San Francisco Tape Music Center. No public storefront; buy/audition their modules in person via Clockface Modular's showroom or order online. Worth seeking out if you want West-Coast-synthesis character with a local pedigree.
music.how_or_when: Order at tokyo-tape-music-center.com or audition at Clockface Modular's Hatagaya showroom. No standalone shop.

music.name: Disk Union — Shinjuku flagship (and branches)
music.detail: Japan's essential used-record empire. The Shinjuku flagship is a multi-floor building organized by genre (Japanese rock/indies in the basement, prog, Latin/Brazil, used rock CDs, indie/alt by floor) with a cafe/bar on 1F. Branches also in Ochanomizu, Shibuya, plus Yokohama/Saitama/Chiba. Deep crates for rock, jazz, electronic and rare Japanese pressings — dig for bargains, though desirable titles sell at market value. Pairs perfectly with the listening-bar scene.
music.how_or_when: Multiple shops near Shinjuku Stn east side; genre-specialist stores are separate buildings. Typically ~11:00-21:00. Check each branch's hours.
music.price_or_cost: Used LPs/CDs from a few hundred yen; rare pressings climb fast. Free to browse.

music.name: e☆earphone Akihabara (headphone/audio building)
music.detail: Japan's premier personal-audio store — an entire building on Akihabara's main street with 3,000+ headphones/earphones/IEMs you can freely demo across all price tiers, plus a CIEM (custom in-ear monitor) section, a Devialet hi-fi corner, and new + checked second-hand stock with trade-ins and repair. The best place in Tokyo to audition monitoring/listening gear for your remote-work studio.
music.how_or_when: Shintou Bldg, 4-7-1 (also listed 4-6-7) Sotokanda, Chiyoda-ku — on Akihabara's main drag, ~3-min walk from Akihabara Stn Electric Town exit. Typically ~11:00-20:00.
music.price_or_cost: Free to demo; gear from ¥2,000 earbuds to ¥500,000+ flagship/CIEM.

music.name: Hard Off (used gear / Junk corner) — Akihabara & Oizumi-Gakuen
music.detail: Nationwide recycle-shop chain and the budget hunter's secret for used synths, drum machines, audio gear, amps and oddball instruments — especially the 'Junk' corner where untested/imperfect items go cheap. Akihabara branch has a solid instrument selection; the Oizumi-Gakuen store is the largest in greater Tokyo for used audio/electronics. Reality check: resellers have driven prices up, so true steals are now rare but still possible. Great low-stakes weekend dig.
music.how_or_when: Akihabara branch near the station; Oizumi-Gakuen on the Seibu Ikebukuro Line. Hit the Junk section first. Stock is luck-of-the-draw — go often.
music.area_or_park: Akihabara / Oizumi-Gakuen (Nerima) and many suburban branches
music.price_or_cost: Junk-corner gear from a few thousand yen; working synths still cheaper than dedicated shops but no longer dirt-cheap.

music.name: Nakano Broadway music floors (Mecano + synthpop digging)
music.detail: Inside the Nakano Broadway mall (anime/toy mecca) sit niche music shops — notably Mecano, a vinyl/CD shop billed as the mecca of Japanese synthpop, tucked among the otaku stores. A fun overlap with your anime/tech-culture interests: dig for Japanese electronic/synthpop records while you're there for the rest of Broadway. Note: some long-standing synth retail in Broadway has come and gone, so treat specific shops as 'verify on arrival.'
music.how_or_when: ~5-min walk from Nakano Stn (JR Chuo/Sobu) via the Sun Mall arcade into Broadway, upper floors. Mall hours vary by shop (~12:00-20:00).
music.price_or_cost: Used records from a few hundred yen up; rarities higher. Free to browse.

music.name: Listening bars / jazz kissa pilgrimage (Bar Martha, Little Soul Cafe, SHeLTeR, DJ Bar Bridge)
music.detail: Tokyo invented the deep-listening bar (jazz kissa / ongaku kissa): hi-fi systems, walls of vinyl, near-silent listening. Best starting points in 2026: Bar Martha (Ebisu) for the classic quiet-listening experience; Little Soul Cafe (Shimokitazawa) for a deep 60s soul/funk collection; DJ Bar Bridge (Shibuya) for an easier, more social first-timer vibe; SHeLTeR (Hachioji) for a serious audiophile pilgrimage further out. A perfect low-key way to study Tokyo's music culture and listening-room acoustics.
music.how_or_when: Evenings; small rooms fill up. Respect listening etiquette (silence during tracks). Confirm each venue's current hours/charge before going — verify closer.
music.price_or_cost: Expect a cover/seat/music charge (varies by venue/event) plus drinks — budget ~¥1,500-¥4,000 per visit. Many enforce quiet and no-photo/no-talking rules.

music.name: Ableton Meetup Tokyo (maker/producer community)
music.detail: Recurring community meetup for electronic-music producers — talks and demos on synthesis, modular-minded software workflows, Ableton Live techniques and chance/generative improvisation, hosted at small Tokyo venues. The single most accessible recurring entry point into Tokyo's music-maker/producer community and exactly aligned with your software + synth interests. Follow on Resident Advisor / social for the next volume's date and venue.
music.how_or_when: Check Resident Advisor (ra.co) and Ableton Meetup Tokyo socials for the next date; events run periodically, usually evenings/weekends. Verify the upcoming 2026 dates closer.
music.price_or_cost: Typically a small door charge / drink minimum (often ~¥1,000-¥2,500); some volumes free. Verify per event.

music.name: Tokyo Festival of Modular (FoM) 2026
music.detail: Tokyo's flagship modular-synth festival: international live acts, manufacturer booths, workshops and a marketplace — a once-a-year gathering of the modular/Eurorack community. Excellent for meeting makers, trying new modules and tapping the scene. 2026 edition is reported around late November; concert venue has been ORD (~4-min walk from Daikanyama Stn). Treat the exact date/venue/price as unconfirmed until the official site/socials post 2026 details.
music.how_or_when: Reported late November 2026. Watch the official FoM site, Facebook (TokyoModularFestival) and YouTube for confirmed dates, venue and tickets — verify closer.
music.area_or_park: Daikanyama / Shibuya area (venue varies by year)
music.price_or_cost: Day/festival passes in past years roughly ¥3,000-¥6,000; workshops may cost extra. Confirm 2026 pricing.

music.name: WHV money gotcha: tax-free shopping + carrying gear home
music.detail: Canadian-WHV-specific note for gear buying: Japan's tax-free (10% consumption-tax exemption) is for SHORT-TERM 'temporary visitor' status only. A Working Holiday visa is a residence status, so you are NOT eligible for tax-free — not on landing day, not in your first weeks. Budget the full 10% on every synth/amp/interface.
music.how_or_when: Assume you pay the 10% consumption tax on all gear. Hunt used stock (Disk Union, second-hand shops) for real savings instead. Keep receipts only for your own records / carrying gear home.
music.price_or_cost: Plan for 10% consumption tax on purchases once you're a resident; possible Canadian import duty on return.

geek.name: Akihabara — Electric Town core (arcades, retro, gachapon, anime)
geek.detail: Your home base district for this whole pillar. Within a 10-min walk: Super Potato (retro), Mandarake Complex, GiGO arcades, Gachapon Kaikan, Radio Kaikan (Steins;Gate landmark), figure/doujinshi/synth shops, plus maid cafes and electronics. Best done as a full afternoon-to-night crawl; come on a weekday to dodge crowds. Note the otaku scene is shifting — some legacy arcades have closed (see GiGO item), so treat any single shop as 'verify it's still there'.
geek.how_or_when: Open daily; most shops 11:00–20:00. Exit at Akihabara Station Electric Town Exit. Free to wander; bring cash (many small shops are cash-only) plus an IC card (Suica/PASMO) for arcades.
geek.price_or_cost: Free to browse; spend what you want

geek.name: Super Potato Akihabara (retro game mecca)
geek.detail: The legendary 3-floor retro shrine: Famicom through Dreamcast carts, consoles, controllers, soundtracks and arcade boards. 3F–4F are sales floors; 5F is the 'Retro Ge-Sen' rooftop-style arcade with classic cabinets you can actually play. Prices on rare/boxed items have climbed with the retro boom, so it's as much a museum as a shop — but mid-tier carts are still gettable.
geek.how_or_when: Open daily ~11:00–20:00, near the Electric Town Exit. Just walk in. Cash preferred for the rare stuff.
geek.price_or_cost: Free entry; loose carts from a few hundred ¥, boxed rarities ¥thousands+

geek.name: GiGO Akihabara arcades (UFO catchers / crane games)
geek.detail: IMPORTANT UPDATE: the iconic GiGO Akihabara 'Building 1' (the old SEGA tower, a landmark since 1992) closed permanently in August 2025. GiGO still runs Buildings 2, 3 and 5 nearby — Building 3 is the go-to for freshly stocked crane-game prizes (anime figures, plush). This is your spot to learn the crane-game meta before hitting bigger arcades.
geek.how_or_when: Open daily, typically ~10:00–23:30. Load money onto an Amusement IC card at in-store machines (credit cards accepted there) and tap to play, or use coins. Verify which buildings are open before a special trip.
geek.price_or_cost: ¥100–200 per crane play; budget ¥1,000–3,000 to actually win something

geek.name: Taito Station & big arcade chains (rhythm/fighting/competitive)
geek.detail: For rhythm games (taiko, maimai, Chunithm, Sound Voltage), fighters, gunner/medal games and competitive cabinets, Taito Station and GiGO multi-floor arcades are the staple. Great recurring after-work outlet near wherever you settle. Akihabara, Shinjuku and Ikebukuro branches are the most stocked.
geek.how_or_when: Open daily, usually until ~23:30–24:00. IC-card tap-to-pay on machines; credit cards work at the charging kiosks. Just walk in.

geek.name: Nakano Broadway (Mandarake mothership + collectibles maze)
geek.detail: A vertical 4-floor warren of 300+ shops with the densest concentration of rare anime/manga goods in Tokyo. Mandarake alone runs 30+ specialty stores here (separate shops for figures, cels, doujinshi, vintage toys, idol goods) — the world's largest secondhand anime retailer. Quieter and cheaper than Akihabara for serious collecting; floors 2–3 are the core.
geek.how_or_when: Complex ~10:00–20:00 but individual shops vary (many noon–20:00, some open at 13:00). 5-min walk up the Sun Mall arcade from Nakano Station North Exit. Free entry.
geek.area_or_park: Nakano (JR Chuo/Tokyo Metro Tozai, ~5 min from Shinjuku)
geek.price_or_cost: Free to browse; collectibles ¥hundreds to ¥tens of thousands

geek.name: Gachapon Kaikan / Akihabara Gachapon Hall (capsule toys)
geek.detail: A single building with 500+ gachapon (capsule-toy) machines — anime characters, weird miniatures (tiny ramen bowls, office supplies, animals in awkward poses). The most efficient one-stop for the capsule-toy ritual. Akihabara has several gachapon halls; this is the flagship.
geek.how_or_when: Open daily, generally ~11:00–20:00 (Gachapon Hall later, ~22:00). Walk in. Bring ¥100 coins — machines are coin-operated; there are change machines on-site.

geek.name: Shibuya PARCO 6F — Nintendo TOKYO + Pokémon Center + Jump Shop + Capcom + Godzilla
geek.detail: Five flagship pop-culture stores on one floor: Nintendo TOKYO (Japan's first official Nintendo store), Pokémon Center Shibuya, Jump Shop (Shonen Jump — One Piece, Dragon Ball, etc.), Capcom Store Tokyo, and the Godzilla Store. The single most efficient stop for first-party gaming/anime merch. Nintendo TOKYO gets queues on weekends.
geek.how_or_when: PARCO open daily ~11:00–21:00. ~5-min walk from Shibuya Station. No ticket needed; on busy days Nintendo TOKYO may issue numbered/timed entry — check the in-mall signage or PARCO site that morning.

geek.name: Pokémon Center Mega Tokyo + Pokémon Cafe (Ikebukuro)
geek.detail: The largest Pokémon Center in Tokyo, inside Sunshine City — broader stock than the Shibuya store, plus the adjacent Pokémon Cafe (themed food, exclusive goods). Good to pair with the Sunshine City arcades/aquarium. There are 4 Pokémon Centers in Tokyo (also Tokyo Station, Skytree); this is the headline one.
geek.how_or_when: Center open daily ~10:00–20:00. The Pokémon Cafe is RESERVATION-ONLY via the official Pokémon Cafe site — book the moment slots open (they release on a rolling ~31-days-ahead basis and sell out fast). Center itself needs no booking.
geek.price_or_cost: Free entry to store; cafe meals ~¥1,500–2,500

geek.name: teamLab Borderless — Azabudai Hills (timed-ticket digital art)
geek.detail: The 'wandering, borderless' digital-art museum — rooms with no fixed map where artworks move between spaces. Different experience from Planets (this one is shoes-on, no water). Dynamic pricing by date/time; weekend evening slots sell out first. This is a permanent flagship (newer than Planets), so less time pressure than Planets/Gundam.
geek.how_or_when: Buy TIMED tickets up to ~2 months ahead on the official site (teamlab.art) — book there first; resellers like Klook can sell out faster. Aim for a weekday slot.

geek.name: teamLab Planets TOKYO — Toyosu (barefoot, water-wading digital art)
geek.detail: The barefoot, immersive one — you wade through knee-deep water and walk on mirrors/moss. Expanded significantly in early 2026 (added Athletic Forest, Future Park, bigger Catching & Collecting Forest). Officially extended through END OF 2027, but it has been extended before, so don't treat the date as urgent — still, it WILL close eventually. Wear/bring shorts (you roll up trousers) and skip white clothing (reflective floors).
geek.how_or_when: Book TIMED tickets in advance on the official site (teamlab.art/e/planets) — sells out on weekends. Closes end of 2027 (verify closer; subject to extension). Yurikamome to Shin-Toyosu, ~1 min walk.

geek.name: Odaiba — Unicorn Gundam statue + Gundam Base + Round1 (DiverCity)
geek.detail: The 19.7m life-size Unicorn Gundam outside DiverCity transforms between Unicorn/Destroy modes with light/sound shows (free). Inside: The Gundam Base Tokyo on 7F (the premier Gunpla shop, exclusive kits) and Round1 Stadium (arcade, bowling, karaoke, sports). TIME-SENSITIVE: the Unicorn Gundam statue's exhibition is set to conclude END OF AUGUST 2026 — go in July/early August if you want to see it.
geek.how_or_when: Statue viewable free anytime; transformation shows run on a daily schedule (check the unicorn-gundam-statue.jp site that day). Go before end of Aug 2026 for the statue. DiverCity ~10:00–21:00; Yurikamome to Daiba or Rinkai Line to Tokyo Teleport.
geek.area_or_park: Odaiba, Koto-ku (DiverCity Tokyo Plaza; Yurikamome/Rinkai Line)
geek.price_or_cost: Statue free; Gunpla kits ¥500–¥10,000+; Round1 pay-per-play

geek.name: Retro game street-crawl: BEEP, Friends, Tokyo Video Gamers, RETRO:G
geek.detail: Beyond Super Potato, a cluster of smaller retro specialists worth a half-day: BEEP (retro PCs, doujin hardware, oddities), Friends (Famicom-era carts and knickknacks), RETRO:G, and Tokyo Video Gamers — a retro gaming BAR where you can drink and play classic consoles for an unlimited-play cover. ('Warpzone' as a named Akihabara shop couldn't be confirmed in current listings — likely closed/renamed; these are the live equivalents.)
geek.how_or_when: Most open ~11:00/12:00–20:00 daily; Tokyo Video Gamers runs into the evening as a bar. Walk in. Cash recommended for shops.
geek.price_or_cost: Free entry; Tokyo Video Gamers cover ~¥1,000–2,000 incl. play

geek.name: Anime pilgrimage (seichi junrei): Suga Shrine + Akihabara landmarks
geek.detail: Top accessible 'seichi junrei' spots: the Suga Shrine staircase in Yotsuya (the iconic final scene of Your Name / Kimi no Na wa) — a real residential staircase you can photograph anytime; pair with the Shibuya scramble/rooftop spots from Weathering With You. In Akihabara, Radio Kaikan and the surrounding streets are core Steins;Gate locations. Cheap, walkable, photo-driven.
geek.how_or_when: Suga Shrine stairs are a public road, open 24h (be quiet — it's residential); shrine grounds ~9:00–17:00, optional ¥500 goshuin seal. Short walk from Yotsuya Station (JR Chuo/Sobu, Marunouchi Line). Akihabara spots anytime. All free.

meetups.name: How Connpass works (the #1 platform for Tokyo tech meetups)
meetups.detail: Connpass is THE dominant platform for Japanese tech/dev/AI study groups (勉強会). Create a free account with email or Google/GitHub login (UI is Japanese-first but Chrome auto-translate works fine; some group pages have an EN toggle). On each event page you click the 参加 (Join) button under a participation tier (参加枠). Two enrollment models: 先着 (first-come, instant confirmation) or 抽選 (lottery, you're entered and notified later). If a tier is full you join the 補欠 (waitlist) — Connpass AUTO-PROMOTES you and emails you when a spot opens, so always waitlist popular events. Etiquette: if you can't attend, cancel your RSVP (キャンセル) so a waitlisted person gets in — repeated no-shows hurt your standing with organizers. Most study groups are free or 500-1000 yen at the door.
meetups.how_or_when: Sign up at connpass.com, then browse the Tokyo calendar (connpass.com/calendar/tokyo/) or follow groups to get event emails. Search keywords like 'AI', 'Go', 'Rust', 'gamedev', 'LT' (lightning talk).
meetups.price_or_cost: Free account; events typically free to ~1000 yen

meetups.name: How Doorkeeper works (English-friendly RSVP system)
meetups.detail: Doorkeeper is the second-most-common platform and is notably more newcomer-friendly than Connpass: the entire interface has a clean English toggle (?locale=en). Create one account and you can RSVP across all communities. Filter by prefecture (Tokyo) to see local events. Many English-language and international communities live here — Tokyo Rubyist Meetup, Business In Japan, Startup Weekend Tokyo, Japan Java User Group, DroidKaigi. Some events charge via the platform; others are free and pay-at-door. Same waitlist concept applies. Tip: a handful of groups use Doorkeeper just to announce, then collect actual RSVP via Google Form — read the event body carefully.
meetups.how_or_when: Register at doorkeeper.jp, set locale to English, browse doorkeeper.jp/prefectures/tokyo/events?locale=en and 'follow' communities to get notified.
meetups.price_or_cost: Free account; events free to a few thousand yen

meetups.name: Tokyo AI (TAI) — Japan's largest AI community
meetups.detail: Over 4,000 members (engineers, researchers, founders, PMs, investors), the biggest AI community in Japan and directly aligned with your build/remote-work and AI interests. Free to join. Runs frequent talks, paper-reading sessions, and networking nights. Operates a bi-weekly newsletter plus topic-based WhatsApp groups: join the Introductions channel, post a short intro, then apply into sub-groups (LLMs, robotics, etc.). Heavily English-friendly given its international membership.
meetups.how_or_when: Join via tokyoai.jp (sign up + newsletter), and follow their event listings on Connpass (tokyoai.connpass.com), Luma (luma.com/tokyo-ai), and Meetup. RSVP through whichever platform a given event uses.
meetups.price_or_cost: Free to join; events usually free

meetups.name: AI Tinkerers Tokyo — hands-on builder/demo nights
meetups.detail: Tokyo chapter of the global AI Tinkerers network (100k+ members across 230+ cities). Format is demo-driven: builders show working AI projects/prototypes, not slideware — ideal if you want to ship side-projects and meet other indie builders while in Japan. English-first. Recent cadence shows monthly-ish meetups in areas like Ginza and Shinagawa.
meetups.how_or_when: Apply to the chapter and RSVP per-event at tokyo.aitinkerers.org (events often require approval; apply early as they cap attendance). Bring a demo to maximize value.
meetups.area_or_park: Ginza / Shinagawa and other central spots
meetups.price_or_cost: Usually free; some events sponsor food/drinks

meetups.name: Tokyo Indies — monthly indie game dev gathering (Akihabara)
meetups.detail: The flagship Tokyo indie-gamedev community and a perfect fit for your games + anime-culture interests. Monthly, casual, English-welcoming. Bring a demo to show, or just watch others present and chat about making games. Held at Mogra, an anime-song bar/club a short walk from Akihabara Station — so it doubles as an otaku-culture night out.
meetups.how_or_when: Third Wednesday of every month in 2026 (per their published schedule). Check tokyoindies.com/en for the date/RSVP each month; arrive early as the venue is intimate.
meetups.price_or_cost: Low — venue entry / one-drink minimum (roughly ~1000 yen + drinks)

meetups.name: Wholesome Indies — cozy indie game dev/creative meetup
meetups.detail: A relaxed, welcoming monthly gathering for indie game developers and enthusiasts to showcase projects in a café setting — friendlier-paced complement to Tokyo Indies. Good for creative collaborators and feedback on works-in-progress.
meetups.how_or_when: Monthly at KEY'S CAFÉ Akihabara SEEKBASE, ~17:00-20:00. RSVP via meetup.com/wholesomeindies. Cost is one café drink (~390 yen+).

meetups.name: Language-specific dev meetups (Go / Python / Ruby / Rust)
meetups.detail: If you're building software remotely, the language-community study groups are the best place to meet working engineers and find collaborators/job leads. Strong, active, English-friendly options: Tokyo Golang Meetup (meetup.com/golang-tokyo), Tokyo Python Meetup (meetup.com/tokyopython, also Discord), Tokyo Rubyist Meetup (trbmeetup.doorkeeper.jp — explicitly bilingual), and Dev Japan (meetup.com/devjapan). Many run lightning-talk (LT) nights where newcomers can present a 5-min talk — a fast way to get known.
meetups.how_or_when: RSVP via each group's platform (Meetup, Doorkeeper, or Connpass). Follow 2-3 and aim for one event/week. Offer to give an LT after a couple of visits.

meetups.name: Tokyo Tech Meetup — bilingual general-tech networking
meetups.detail: An international community where tech, product, design, and startup people gather. Format: discussion of recent Japanese tech news, curated 3-5 minute lightning talks, and open networking — run bilingually in Japanese and English. Good general-purpose entry point and a place to practice presenting.
meetups.how_or_when: Follow and RSVP at tokyotechmeetup.connpass.com (also listed on Meetup). Events post a few weeks ahead; popular ones fill, so waitlist if needed.

meetups.name: Vibe Coders Tokyo / Melting Hack — AI-build & hackathon scene
meetups.detail: For shipping AI side-projects and meeting other builders: Vibe Coders Tokyo runs AI-project build nights (RSVP via Luma: luma.com/vibecoderstokyo), and Melting Hack runs AI hackathons (melting-hack.tokyo). DataMinds Tokyo (luma.com/data_minds_tokyo) covers data & AI. Luma is a third RSVP platform you'll encounter — simple email/Google sign-in, one-click register, calendar invite emailed to you.
meetups.how_or_when: Create a Luma account, follow these calendars, RSVP per event. Hackathons need earlier sign-up and sometimes team formation in advance.
meetups.price_or_cost: Free to low; hackathons sometimes ticketed

meetups.name: Tenkey Tokyo — keyboard / hardware maker meetup
meetups.detail: A custom mechanical-keyboard and hardware-tinkerer community — a strong fit for your music-gear/synth/maker side. Hands-on culture overlapping with the DIY synth and electronics scene. English how-to-join page exists.
meetups.how_or_when: See tenkey.tokyo/en/how-to-join/ for the current join/RSVP method (Discord + event sign-ups). Verify next meetup date closer to your arrival.

meetups.name: Tokyo Festival of Modular — synth & modular community anchor
meetups.detail: Annual festival where modular-synth artists, sound designers, and engineers gather for concerts, workshops, and an exhibitor area — the best single entry point into Tokyo's synth/electronic-music maker community, directly matching your music-gear interest. Concerts at ORD, ~4 min from Daikanyama Station. Use it to meet the people who run the smaller monthly synth jams and shop events (e.g., Five G, Tokyo's famed synth shop).
meetups.how_or_when: Held late November 2026 (verify closer — annual but dates shift). Watch the festival site/socials and Tokyo Cheapo's listing for tickets and the workshop schedule.

meetups.name: Language exchange — Mundo Lingo Tokyo & meetup groups
meetups.detail: Best low-pressure way to build a social circle fast and practice Japanese. Mundo Lingo Tokyo runs free, bar-based mixer nights (you wear flag stickers for languages you speak/learn; sustained by bar drink sales, no entry fee). Plus weekly recurring exchanges: Wednesday English⇔Japanese and Saturday Spanish⇔Japanese events run by Tokyo meetup groups, beginner-friendly across Tokyo. For sheer volume, Tokyo International Friends & Events (TIFE) is the largest international community (35,000+ members, 50+ events/month).
meetups.how_or_when: Mundo Lingo: follow facebook.com/mundolingotokyo for the current weekly venue/time. TIFE/others: RSVP at meetup.com/international-meetup-tokyo and meetup.com/tokyo-international-friends. Just show up; buy a drink.
meetups.price_or_cost: Free entry (buy your own drinks); some Meetup events ~500-1500 yen

meetups.name: Conventions to anchor your year (book RSVP/tickets early)
meetups.detail: Big annual gatherings that fuse your games/anime/indie-dev interests and are great for meeting the wider community in one shot: Tokyo Indie Games Summit (TIGS) and Tokyo Game Show (TGS, Makuhari Messe, every September). TIGS 2026 already ran (Mar 20-21, 2026 at IMAGINUS) — so target TIGS 2027 (~March 2027) and TGS 2026 (September 2026) during your stay. These also list affiliated meetups and after-parties you can RSVP to via Connpass/Doorkeeper.
meetups.how_or_when: TGS 2026: buy public-day tickets when they go on sale mid-2026 (verify dates). TIGS 2027: watch indiegamessummit.tokyo and @TOKYO_IGS for the spring-2027 announcement. Pair with the monthly Tokyo Indies meetup year-round.
meetups.price_or_cost: TGS public day ~2000-2700 yen; TIGS public day ticketed

meetups.name: Newcomer etiquette & WHV-specific gotchas for meetups
meetups.detail: Practical rules so you integrate smoothly: (1) Always cancel RSVPs you can't make — Japanese organizers track no-shows and waitlisted people are counting on the spot. (2) Many study-group events ask for a 懇親会 (after-party) fee (~2000-4000 yen, izakaya) collected separately — opt in for the real networking. (3) Bring a name/contact card or your GitHub/socials; exchanging contacts is expected. (4) Volunteering to give a 5-min lightning talk (LT) is the fastest path to being remembered. (5) WHV note: the Working Holiday visa lets you attend/network freely, but if a 'hackathon' or meetup offers paid contract work, ensure it fits your visa's work rules and that you've handled your residence registration, My Number, and pension/tax enrollment before taking on paid gigs — keep proof-of-funds and tax docs tidy since some startup events can lead to job offers.
meetups.how_or_when: Apply from day one of joining any platform. Confirm visa/work specifics with the immigration/ward office during your first weeks in Tokyo.
meetups.price_or_cost: After-parties ~2000-4000 yen when you opt in

livemusic.name: Womb
livemusic.detail: Tokyo's flagship big-room techno/house club — Funktion-One-style sound, lasers, and a steady stream of international techno/house headliners. The default for a serious electronic night out.
livemusic.how_or_when: CLUB · Thu–Sun nightly; weekends ~23:00–morning · Door or RA advance; bring passport ID
livemusic.price_or_cost: ~¥3,000–4,500 door (more for big international guests); cheaper before midnight / with flyer or RA advance

livemusic.name: Vent
livemusic.detail: Dark, no-photos, sound-first basement built for deep/minimal techno and house heads. The closest spirit-successor to the old Contact for discerning electronic listeners.
livemusic.how_or_when: CLUB · Fri & Sat, doors ~23:00 til ~06:00 · Door or RA advance; passport ID required

livemusic.name: Circus Tokyo
livemusic.detail: Intimate, low-ceilinged room with a cult following for leftfield techno, house, bass and live electronic acts. Great for catching rising local and touring artists up close.
livemusic.how_or_when: CLUB · Fri/Sat + scattered weeknights; check RA per event · Door or RA advance
livemusic.area_or_park: Shibuya (Shibuya Stn, New South Exit ~5 min)

livemusic.name: Aoyama Hachi
livemusic.detail: Two-floor club spanning house, techno, dub, bass and afro/global nights. Reliable mid-size venue for digging into Tokyo's underground residencies.
livemusic.how_or_when: CLUB · 6 nights/week; genre varies by night — check RA · Door or RA advance

livemusic.name: Aoyama Tunnel
livemusic.detail: Tiny, hidden underground bar-club for serious selectors — house, disco, ambient and experimental. A connoisseur's late-night spot.
livemusic.how_or_when: DJ BAR · Recurring 'TUNNEL FRIDAY' + weekend events · Door or RA; small room, go early

livemusic.name: Mitsuki
livemusic.detail: Compact basement room with a strong house/techno/disco programming streak — good for an unpretentious, dance-floor-focused weekend night.
livemusic.how_or_when: CLUB · Fri/Sat weekly; check RA · Door or RA advance
livemusic.area_or_park: Shibuya (B1, Dogenzaka, Scramble area; Shibuya Stn)

livemusic.name: Solfa
livemusic.detail: Cozy Nakameguro basement known for deep house, techno and minimal in a warm, intimate setting — a more relaxed alternative to the Shibuya crush.
livemusic.how_or_when: CLUB · Fri/Sat + select weeknights; check RA · Door or RA advance

livemusic.name: DJ Bar Oath
livemusic.detail: Late-night DJ bar with a mirror-ball room and free-spirited house/disco/techno sets — a cheap, no-commitment way to end (or start) a night out.
livemusic.how_or_when: DJ BAR · Open most nights; different DJ event nightly · Walk in
livemusic.price_or_cost: Low cover (~¥1,000–2,000) or free early; cheap drinks

livemusic.name: DJ Bar Koara
livemusic.detail: Beloved tiny basement bar near Tower Records with sharp resident selectors across house, disco and broken beat. Big-name guests sometimes drop in for off-nights.
livemusic.how_or_when: DJ BAR · Weekends + recurring monthly residents; occasional intl guests · Walk in / check Instagram
livemusic.area_or_park: Shibuya (Jinnan 1-13-15, Koritsu Bldg B1F; Shibuya Stn)

livemusic.name: Bar Bonobo
livemusic.detail: Converted old house turned multi-floor party spot with a rooftop — eclectic house, disco, dub and global sounds in an intimate, social atmosphere.
livemusic.how_or_when: DJ BAR · Frequent weekend DJ nights / events · Walk in / check Instagram

livemusic.name: Forestlimit
livemusic.detail: DIY experimental hub for noise, ambient, leftfield electronics, art shows and weirdo dance parties — the place to plug into Tokyo's underground/maker fringe.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Irregular — KATO Massacre experimental night every Wed; noise/disco/ambient/dance events throughout; check IG @forestlimit_info / RA · Door / check Instagram or RA per event
livemusic.area_or_park: Hatagaya (1F, K3 Bldg, 2-8-15 Hatagaya, Shibuya-ku; Hatagaya Stn)

livemusic.name: Super Dommune
livemusic.detail: Japan's legendary live-streaming studio-venue mixing electronic music, talks and culture nightly. Catch the broadcasts, then snag rare in-person tickets for special sessions.
livemusic.how_or_when: VENUE · Webcasts mostly weekday evenings ~19:00–24:00; some in-person/ticketed specials · Watch live stream online; check site for ticketed events
livemusic.price_or_cost: Free to stream; ticketed for in-person specials

livemusic.name: DJ Bar Bridge
livemusic.detail: DJ bar with a postcard view over Shibuya Crossing and a soundtrack spanning house, jazz and electronic — a refined, listening-focused vantage point on the city.
livemusic.how_or_when: LISTENING BAR · Nightly; DJ-led vinyl/electronic and jazzy sets · Walk in / reservation for window seats
livemusic.area_or_park: Shibuya (10F, overlooking Scramble Crossing; Shibuya Stn)

livemusic.name: WWW & WWW X
livemusic.detail: Former cinema turned two-room live house — the city's best mid-size stage for indie, electronic, post-rock and touring acts, plus host to MUTEK and Synchronicity showcases.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights; check monthly schedule · e+ / Ticket Pia / RA / door
livemusic.area_or_park: Shibuya (Udagawacho, near Center Gai; Shibuya Stn)

livemusic.name: Liquidroom
livemusic.detail: ~900-cap room that's a sweet spot for international indie and electronic touring acts, with excellent sound and the attached Time Out Cafe bar.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights; live gigs + occasional all-night club events · e+ / Ticket Pia / RA / door

livemusic.name: Unit
livemusic.detail: Three-level basement complex (Unit + Saloon + Unice) that flips from indie/electronic gigs to late club nights — a core venue for forward-thinking bookings.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights; live early, club/DJ late on weekends · e+ / Ticket Pia / RA / door

livemusic.name: Spotify O-East
livemusic.detail: Large multi-floor live house anchoring Shibuya's club street — hosts indie/rock/electronic headliners and festival showcases (MUTEK evening shows).
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights · e+ / Ticket Pia / door

livemusic.name: Spotify O-nest
livemusic.detail: Small, intimate top-floor room ideal for indie, experimental and emerging electronic acts — go here to find what's next.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights · e+ / Ticket Pia / door
livemusic.area_or_park: Shibuya (Dogenzaka, top of the O complex; Shibuya Stn)

livemusic.name: Shibuya Club Quattro
livemusic.detail: Long-running ~700-cap room with a strong indie and international booking pedigree and clean sightlines/sound — a reliable bet for touring indie acts.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights; hosts festival showcases (e.g. Synchronicity) · e+ / Ticket Pia / door
livemusic.area_or_park: Shibuya (top of Center Gai; Shibuya Stn)

livemusic.name: Shimokitazawa Shelter
livemusic.detail: Tiny, sweaty basement that's the spiritual heart of Shimokita's indie/punk circuit — the place to see Japanese guitar bands at arm's length.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights · e+ / door

livemusic.name: Shimokitazawa Basement Bar & Three
livemusic.detail: Two adjoining basement rooms covering indie gigs and DJ/bar nights — a dense, scene-defining node for discovering local bands and selectors.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights; Three flips between gig and DJ/bar nights · e+ / door

livemusic.name: Shimokitazawa Era
livemusic.detail: Small underground room with an adventurous booking policy spanning indie, post-rock and experimental — good for off-the-radar bills.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights · e+ / door

livemusic.name: Club 251
livemusic.detail: Veteran Shimokita live house for indie/rock bills — rounds out a neighbourhood you can pub-crawl between venues in one night.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights · e+ / door

livemusic.name: Koenji High
livemusic.detail: Mid-size room anchoring Koenji's gritty DIY/punk/indie scene — pair with the neighbourhood's record shops and dive bars for a full day out.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights · e+ / door

livemusic.name: Niman Den-atsu (20000V)
livemusic.detail: Successor to the legendary 20000V — raw noise, punk, hardcore and experimental. Essential for the harder, weirder end of the live spectrum.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Nightly bills · Door / check schedule

livemusic.name: Shinjuku Loft
livemusic.detail: Historic live house (since the '70s) with main and bar stages running parallel bills — a Kabukicho institution for rock, indie and punk.
livemusic.how_or_when: LIVE HOUSE · Most nights; two-stage events (main + Bar stage) · e+ / Ticket Pia / door

livemusic.name: Blue Note Tokyo
livemusic.detail: Tokyo's premier jazz room, drawing world-class jazz, soul and electronic-leaning artists. Splurge-worthy for a marquee name in an intimate seated setting.
livemusic.how_or_when: JAZZ · Nightly; typically two sets (~17:00/19:00 & ~20:00/21:30) — check per artist · Reserve online (members/general); arrive early for seating

livemusic.name: Cotton Club
livemusic.detail: Blue Note's sister room — slightly more intimate, with a programming streak that wanders into soul, nu-jazz and electronic-adjacent artists.
livemusic.how_or_when: JAZZ · Most nights; first show ~17:00–19:00, usually two sets · Reserve online

livemusic.name: Shinjuku Pit Inn
livemusic.detail: The serious-listener's jazz club — focused on Japanese avant-garde, free and modern jazz at very fair prices. No frills, all music.
livemusic.how_or_when: JAZZ · Daily; daytime set ~14:30 and evening set ~19:30/20:00 · Door / check schedule

livemusic.name: Body & Soul
livemusic.detail: Intimate live-jazz room run by a legendary host, spotlighting top Japanese players in a warm, dinner-club setting.
livemusic.how_or_when: JAZZ · Most nights; two sets ~20:00 & ~21:45 (one charge usually covers both) · Reserve by phone/online
livemusic.area_or_park: Shibuya (moved from Omotesando Oct 2021; Shibuya Stn)

livemusic.name: Jazz SPOT Intro — all-night Saturday jam
livemusic.detail: Cramped, smoky basement hosting Tokyo's famous all-night Saturday jam session — players rotate til dawn. A only-in-Tokyo jazz rite of passage.
livemusic.how_or_when: JAZZ · Saturday all-night jam ~17:00 til ~05:00 (first trains); other jams midweek · Walk in

livemusic.name: Dug (Jazz Cafe & Bar)
livemusic.detail: Storied Shinjuku jazz-kissa (a Murakami haunt) — vintage decor, deep vinyl, and a no-pressure way to settle in with a coffee or cocktail and listen.
livemusic.how_or_when: JAZZ · Daily ~12:00–23:30; jazz-kissa by day, bar by night · Walk in
livemusic.area_or_park: Shinjuku (B1, Yasukuni-dori, near east exit; Shinjuku Stn)
livemusic.price_or_cost: Drink prices only (no heavy cover); records + occasional live

livemusic.name: Eagle (jazz kissa)
livemusic.detail: Textbook old-school jazz-kissa: order a coffee, stay silent, and let a hand-built hi-fi rig and decades-deep record wall do the talking. Pure listening ritual.
livemusic.how_or_when: JAZZ · Daily; talking prohibited until 18:00, light conversation after · Walk in

livemusic.name: JBS (Jazz, Blues, Soul)
livemusic.detail: One-man bar where owner Kobayashi-san spins from ~10,000 LPs of jazz, blues and soul. A hushed, vinyl-purist sanctuary in the middle of Shibuya.
livemusic.how_or_when: LISTENING BAR · Evenings; quiet listening · Walk in (tiny — go early)

livemusic.name: Bar Martha
livemusic.detail: Archetypal Tokyo listening bar — tube amps, vintage horns, candlelight and a strict no-requests policy. The template the global 'listening bar' trend copied.
livemusic.how_or_when: LISTENING BAR · Nightly; vinyl played loud-and-detailed, conversation kept low, no requests · Walk in

livemusic.name: Grandfather's
livemusic.detail: Long-running rock/soul listening bar with a warm vintage system and a wide, crowd-pleasing record wall — an easy first listening-bar to break the ice.
livemusic.how_or_when: LISTENING BAR · 17:00–02:00, closed Mondays · Walk in
livemusic.area_or_park: Shibuya (B1F, Flat Bldg, 1-24-7 Shibuya; Shibuya Stn)

livemusic.name: Studio Mule
livemusic.detail: Run by the Mule Musiq label — hi-fi listening, natural wine and an attached record shop. A tastemaker hangout bridging the listening-bar and electronic worlds.
livemusic.how_or_when: LISTENING BAR · Evenings; hi-fi listening + small record shop & wine · Walk in

livemusic.name: Ginza Music Bar
livemusic.detail: Upscale, design-forward listening bar with an audiophile rig — a polished, date-worthy contrast to the gritty Shibuya kissa.
livemusic.how_or_when: LISTENING BAR · Evenings; curated vinyl on a high-fidelity system, 3,000+ records · Reserve recommended

livemusic.name: Wax Poetic & Epulor
livemusic.detail: Pair of Nakameguro listening spots blending jazz, soul and electronic on serious vintage systems — central to the neighbourhood's hi-fi bar cluster.
livemusic.how_or_when: LISTENING BAR · Evenings; vintage audio + deep records (Wax Poetic); DJ across jazz/soul/electronic (Epulor) · Walk in / check IG

livemusic.name: Tokyo Record Bar
livemusic.detail: Novel 'vinyl omakase' — a host curates a multi-course record set for the room, with each guest also picking a track. A fun, social one-off experience.
livemusic.how_or_when: LISTENING BAR · Seatings by booking; communal 'vinyl omakase' format · Reserve online in advance
livemusic.price_or_cost: Set price per seating (food/drink + vinyl program)

livemusic.name: Tokyo Festival of Modular (FoM)
livemusic.detail: Japan's flagship modular-synth gathering — hands-on gear from makers, performances and workshops. The single best event for the modular/maker lane. Verify exact 2026 dates closer.
livemusic.how_or_when: FESTIVAL · Annual, late November · Advance tickets via festival site / Peatix; verify dates closer
livemusic.area_or_park: Daikanyama (concerts at ORD ~4 min from Daikanyama Stn) + exhibitor space
livemusic.price_or_cost: Day/weekend passes (~¥3,000–6,000); free-ish exhibitor browsing varies

livemusic.name: Fuji Rock Festival '26
livemusic.detail: Japan's biggest and best-curated festival — sprawling mountain site with a deep electronic/leftfield presence (Red Marquee, Field of Heaven) alongside indie and global headliners.
livemusic.how_or_when: FESTIVAL · Fri–Sun, 24–26 Jul 2026 (3-day) · e+ / official site / Ticket Pia
livemusic.area_or_park: Naeba Ski Resort, Niigata (~2.5h from Tokyo by shinkansen+bus)

livemusic.name: Summer Sonic 2026 (Tokyo)
livemusic.detail: Urban festival with a strong pop/indie/rock bill and a dedicated dance stage (Sonic Stage / Sonicmania all-nighter) for electronic acts. Easy day-trip from central Tokyo.

livemusic.area_or_park: Chiba / Makuhari (ZOZO Marine + Makuhari Messe) — not Tokyo proper

livemusic.name: Ultra Japan 2026
livemusic.detail: Big-stage EDM/house/techno festival in the city — mainstream and commercial, but a spectacle and an easy in-town electronic weekend.

livemusic.area_or_park: Tokyo — Odaiba Ultra Park (Aomi/Daiba; Yurikamome or Rinkai Line)

livemusic.name: FFKT 2026 (formerly Taicoclub)
livemusic.detail: Tastemaker outdoor festival blending forward-thinking electronic, ambient and live acts on a scenic seaside site. A core date for the discerning electronic crowd. Verify dates closer.

livemusic.area_or_park: Izu Shimoda, Shizuoka — Hotel Izukyu, Shirahama Beach (~3 hr via Odoriko ltd express)

livemusic.name: The Labyrinth 2026 (Mindgames)
livemusic.detail: Legendary, intimate deep-/minimal-techno camping festival in the mountains with a famously pristine sound system and a no-frills, music-devotee ethos. A bucket-list rave. Verify dates closer.

livemusic.name: MUTEK.JP 2026 (Edition 11)
livemusic.detail: The city's premier festival for audiovisual and experimental electronic performance — exactly the cutting-edge synth/AV programming this lane lives for. Verify 2026 dates closer.

livemusic.name: Blue Note Jazz Festival in Japan 2026
livemusic.detail: Arena-scale jazz/soul/R&B festival from the Blue Note team — a concentrated dose of marquee jazz and soul-adjacent acts in one weekend. Verify dates closer.

livemusic.area_or_park: Tokyo — Ariake Arena, Koto (Yurikamome / Rinkai Line, Toyosu side)

livemusic.name: Tokyo Jazz Festival (東京JAZZ)
livemusic.detail: Long-running flagship jazz festival spanning straight-ahead, fusion and crossover/electronic-jazz acts. A reliable annual anchor for the jazz lane. Verify dates closer.
livemusic.how_or_when: FESTIVAL · Historically late Aug / early autumn over several days (verify exact 2026 dates) · e+ / Ticket Pia / official site

livemusic.name: OHJO Rave NYE
livemusic.detail: Multi-floor Kabukicho NYE rave — a fun, central, all-night way to bring in the new year on the dance floor. Verify 2026 edition closer.

livemusic.name: Rainbow Disco Club 2027 (RDC)
livemusic.detail: Beloved open-air house/disco/techno festival on a scenic Izu plateau, with stellar international lineups (recent years: Four Tet, Gilles Peterson, Kode9). A spring highlight. Verify 2027 dates closer.

livemusic.area_or_park: Higashi-Izu, Shizuoka — Higashi-Izu Cross Country Course (~2.5 hr)

livemusic.name: Synchronicity Festival 2027
livemusic.detail: Multi-venue Shibuya circuit festival spanning indie, electronic and crossover acts — wristband-hop between rooms in one neighbourhood. Verify 2027 dates closer.
livemusic.how_or_when: FESTIVAL · Mid-Apr 2027 (2026 ran 11–12 Apr Sat–Sun + Fri pre-party — verify closer) · e+ / official site
livemusic.area_or_park: Shibuya, Tokyo — multi-venue (WWW, WWW X, O-East area) + pre-party at Duo Music Exchange

livemusic.name: Tokyo Gig Guide
livemusic.detail: English-language gig listings aggregator — the easiest way to keep a weekly pulse on indie/live shows you'd otherwise miss behind Japanese-only schedules.
livemusic.how_or_when: MEETUP · Ongoing; check weekly · Browse listings online; many link to English ticketing

livemusic.name: Resident Advisor (RA) — Tokyo events
livemusic.detail: The essential map for the electronic/club lane — every venue above lists nights here, and advance RA tickets are often cheaper than the door. Set up alerts for your favourite venues.
livemusic.how_or_when: MEETUP · Ongoing; check weekly · RA app/site — filter by Tokyo, follow venues/artists
livemusic.price_or_cost: Free to browse; advance tickets via RA

livemusic.name: e+ / Lawson Ticket / Ticket Pia (how to buy)
livemusic.detail: The three domestic ticketing platforms most live houses and festivals use — knowing how to navigate them (and konbini pickup) unlocks shows that never reach international sellers.
livemusic.how_or_when: MEETUP · Ongoing · e+ (eplus.jp), Lawson Ticket (l-tike.com), Ticket Pia (t.pia.jp); pay/collect at konbini

rooms.name: UNPLAN Kagurazaka — design hostel (soft landing)
rooms.rent: Nightly from ~¥5,000 (~$33); ask about weekly/long-stay rate
rooms.fees: Free Wi-Fi; in-room washer/dryer + microwave; on-site cafe
rooms.moveIn: Book online from abroad; same-day possible
rooms.contact: English-speaking staff; book via own site or Hostelworld/Booking
rooms.note: Stylish, sociable soft-landing hostel in a great central neighborhood, with in-room laundry (handy for a multi-week stay) and a cafe to work from. No published monthly plan — ask for a long-stay/weekly rate, or just book nightly while you secure a share house. Verify current rates at booking.

rooms.name: UR danchi — public rental (no key money / no guarantor / no agent fee)
rooms.area: Citywide Kanto (Tokyo 23-ward danchi + Kawasaki, Yokohama, Saitama, Chiba)
rooms.station: Various — many danchi are a bus ride from the nearest station
rooms.fees: Utilities billed separately; no renewal fee, no agent fee, no guarantor-company fee
rooms.oneTime: No key money, no agent fee — only deposit
rooms.moveIn: After arrival only — needs a residence card in hand, so not bookable from abroad
requirements: Residence card (issued on arrival at the airport for WHV)
No guarantor and no guarantor company required
Income roughly 4x monthly rent annually, or savings/lump-sum-rent alternative
Apply in person or online at a UR center
rooms.contact: UR rental centers; English-speaking agents via partners (e.g. ur-housing.com / WhiteStone)
rooms.note: The classic low-upfront route: UR formally waives key money, guarantor, agent commission AND renewal fees on all units — a national policy, not case-by-case. Trade-off: stock is mostly older 1970s–90s buildings and you must already hold a residence card, so line this up for month 1–2 after landing, not before. The income/savings test is the main gate for a new WHV arrival; UR accepts a lump-sum rent prepayment as a savings alternative.

rooms.name: Village House — budget apartments (no key money / no deposit / no fees)
rooms.area: Nationwide incl. greater Tokyo / Kanto (often outer wards & suburbs)
rooms.station: Various — many estates are suburban, bus-or-bike from the station
rooms.rent: Tokyo 1R from ~¥100,000 / mo; nationwide rents start ~¥20,000 / mo
rooms.fees: No renewal fee, no handling fee; utilities separate
rooms.oneTime: No key money, no agency fee
rooms.deposit: Usually none — but a deposit may apply depending on contract/screening
rooms.moveIn: Rolling; English call centre (0120-266-078) and email intake
requirements: Often no guarantor / no guarantor company — but a joint guarantor can be requested depending on screening
Foreign residents accepted on all properties
rooms.contact: English support via call centre + email; 'gaijin-friendly' branding
rooms.note: Lowest-possible upfront cost: explicitly no key money, no deposit (usually), no renewal/handling fee, all units open to foreigners. The catch is location — the cheapest stock is suburban/outer-Kanto, and Tokyo-proper 1R starts nearer ¥100k. Guarantor is frequently waived but not guaranteed; budget for a guarantor-company fee in case screening asks for one. Verify current Tokyo availability and the exact deposit/guarantor terms on the unit before committing.

rooms.name: MetroResidences — furnished serviced apartments (no guarantor)
rooms.area: Central Tokyo (Minato, Shibuya, Shinjuku and surrounding wards)
rooms.fees: Internet + utilities included (with cap); cleaning fee
rooms.oneTime: No key money, no agent fee
rooms.deposit: One refundable month's deposit + cleaning fee (~half the initial cost vs a normal lease)
rooms.moveIn: Bookable from abroad (online / email booking.jp@metroresidences.com); 30-day notice to leave
requirements: Valid ID / passport (requirements vary by unit)
rooms.note: A polished from-abroad soft-landing into a real private apartment (not a share house): furnished, utilities/internet bundled, no guarantor, and you can sign before you fly. Price is the trade-off — it sits at the splurge end (¥180k+), so it suits the first 1–6 months while you set up a residence card and bank account, then move to UR/Village House for cheaper long-term rent. Longer stays unlock a half-month-free-per-6-months discount. Verify current rates and availability — pricing scales with lease length.

rooms.name: Leopalace21 — monthly furnished mansions (no guarantor)
rooms.fees: Many units include free water + utilities; internet often included
rooms.oneTime: No agency fee; key money/deposit waived on many units (filterable)
rooms.deposit: Varies — filter for 'no security deposit / no key money' units
rooms.moveIn: Rolling; online consultation + e-contract, English site
rooms.contact: English/Chinese/Korean website with online consultation & contract
rooms.note: The big-network monthly option: furnished studios with appliances, free utilities on many units, no guarantor, and screening that weighs ability-to-pay over Japanese ability — genuinely foreigner-easy. Reputation caveat: Leopalace is known for thin walls and variable build quality, so read recent unit reviews. Monthly-plan rates run higher per month than a standard year lease; good as a flexible 1–6 month base. Verify the per-unit key-money/deposit and current monthly rate, which fluctuate.

rooms.name: Apartment Japan — book a private apartment from abroad (no guarantor, no JP bank)
rooms.fees: Many units state no agency fee; initial costs shown transparently before booking
rooms.oneTime: Many properties explicitly no key money, no agency fee
rooms.deposit: Varies by unit — displayed up front
rooms.moveIn: Fully remote — search, quote, apply and e-sign (DocuSign) before arrival; min 1 month
requirements: No Japanese guarantor / no guarantee company enrolment
No Japanese bank account — uses your home-country credit card as security
rooms.contact: 24/7 English support; entire process online incl. e-signature
rooms.note: The cleanest sign-before-you-fly route into a private apartment: no guarantor, no Japanese bank account, and the lease is e-signed via DocuSign from the US using a home-country credit card as the security mechanism. Furnished options exist (rare in Japan). Premium over local-market rent is the cost of that convenience. Ideal for locking housing before you land, then optionally switching to UR/Village House once you have a residence card and bank account. Verify the per-unit initial cost and current availability before booking.

rooms.name: AtHearth — foreigner-focused agency (guarantor-company route, apply from abroad)
rooms.rent: Market rent; examples ~¥172,000–390,000 / mo (full catalogue runs lower for studios)
rooms.fees: Standard agency move-in costs; optional paid concierge add-ons
rooms.oneTime: Filter of 2,400+ 'no key money' listings; guarantor-company fee applies instead of a personal guarantor
rooms.moveIn: Apply from abroad via their platform; start ~2–3 months out, viewings ~3 weeks out, sign 1–2 weeks before
requirements: No personal Japanese guarantor — a guarantor company (hoshō gaisha) is arranged instead
rooms.contact: English digital platform + agents; 10+ years serving expats
rooms.note: An English-first brokerage rather than a single landlord: it sources normal market apartments and clears the two foreigner blockers for you — it arranges a guarantor company (hoshō gaisha) so you need no personal Japanese guarantor, and has a large 'no key money' filter. You can start the process from Canada. Because these are regular market leases, expect a standard guarantor-company fee (~¥30,000–60,000 + annual renewal) and normal move-in costs; this buys you a long-term unbranded apartment rather than a serviced one. Verify the specific unit's key-money/deposit and the guarantor-company fee before signing.

rooms.name: Wagaya Japan — multilingual no-guarantor apartment portal
rooms.fees: Maintenance fee ~¥15,000/mo on many units; utilities separate
rooms.oneTime: Many listings show key money ¥0 (deposit/key shown as 0mo/0mo)
rooms.moveIn: Multilingual 'request a property search' intake; English interface for remote enquiry
requirements: Multilingual: EN / 简体 / 繁體 / Tiếng Việt / 日本語
rooms.contact: Multilingual listing portal with property-search request form
rooms.note: A listing portal (not a landlord) with a built-in 'no guarantor' filter and very low advertised rents — some studios show ¥0 deposit / ¥0 key money. Watch the headline numbers: the cheapest figures often carry a ~¥15,000/mo maintenance fee and tiny units, and remote-application terms aren't spelled out, so confirm per-unit. Strong multilingual support makes it an easy place to short-list no-guarantor units. Verify the unit's true all-in monthly cost (rent + maintenance), deposit, and whether they'll process a from-abroad application.
